events.text: The patient is a 55-year-old male named Marcus R. Ellison. He is White/Non-Hispanic, born in 1969, with a history of intermittent sun exposure and outdoor occupational work as a landscaper. No prior personal history of melanoma. Family history notable for a maternal aunt with melanoma diagnosed in her 60s. He is a former smoker (quit 12 years ago, 10 pack-year history) with no significant alcohol use. Performance status ECOG 1 at time of metastatic diagnosis.

At age 55 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) MELANOMA, MALIGNANT (ICD-O-3 histology code 8720/3), primary site: SKIN OF BACK (ICD-O-3 site code C44.5). TNM staging: T4b N3 M1c. Summary stage: Distant. The primary lesion was a 4.2 cm ulcerated nodular melanoma on the upper back. Mitotic rate: >4/mm2. Clark level: V. Breslow thickness: 6.1 mm. Ulceration: present. Regional lymph node involvement: bilateral axillary and left cervical lymphadenopathy (>4 nodes involved). Distant metastases: multiple hepatic lesions (largest 3.1 cm), multiple cutaneous and subcutaneous satellite nodules on trunk and extremities, right axillary nodal mass 4.8 cm. LDH: 642 U/L (elevated, 2x ULN). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY. Tumor Size: 42 mm primary.

At age 55 years, the patient had an anatomic pathology result from excisional biopsy of the primary back lesion and core needle biopsy of the right axillary lymph node. PRIMARY LESION (back): Nodular melanoma, Breslow thickness 6.1 mm, Clark level V, ulceration present, mitotic rate 6/mm2, perineural invasion absent, lymphovascular invasion present. Margins: positive (deep margin). LYMPH NODE (right axilla, core biopsy): Metastatic melanoma, effacement of nodal architecture, extranodal extension present. Immunohistochemistry: S100 positive, HMB-45 positive, Melan-A positive, SOX10 positive, Ki-67 approximately 40%. BRAF V600E immunohistochemistry: strongly positive (VE1 antibody). PD-L1 expression (28-8 assay): tumor proportion score (TPS) 25%, combined positive score (CPS) 38.

At age 55 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for metastatic melanoma based on a specimen obtained from the right axillary lymph node metastasis (FFPE tissue). The assay was a comprehensive solid tumor panel (523-gene panel). Findings: BRAF p.V600E mutation (allele frequency 48.2%, oncogenic, actionable - BRAF/MEK inhibitor sensitivity); CDKN2A homozygous deletion (loss of tumor suppressor); PTEN p.R130* nonsense mutation (loss of function, PI3K pathway activation, allele frequency 41.1%); TP53 p.R175H missense mutation (gain-of-function, allele frequency 39.8%); TERT promoter c.-124C>T mutation (allele frequency 52.3%, associated with poor prognosis in melanoma); RAC1 p.P29S missense mutation (allele frequency 11.4%, GTPase activating, UV signature); NF1 wild-type; RAS (NRAS, KRAS, HRAS) wild-type; KIT wild-type; GNAQ/GNA11 wild-type. Copy number alterations: CDKN2A homozygous deletion (chr9p21), CCND1 amplification (chr11q13), MYC amplification (chr8q24). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 18.4 mutations/megabase (intermediate-high). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Mutational signature: UV signature dominant (SBS7a, SBS7b). No actionable fusions detected. PD-L1 expression by NGS-based inference: consistent with TPS 25%. Germline confirmation pending (constitutional BRAF not suspected given somatic VAF pattern).

At age 55 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (staging), which showed cancer. Findings: Multiple bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, largest right axillary conglomerate mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 cm with central necrosis. Left cervical level II and III lymph nodes enlarged (up to 2.2 cm). Liver: six discrete hypodense lesions, largest in segment VII measuring 3.1 x 2.7 cm and segment II measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm, with enhancement pattern consistent with melanoma metastases. No pulmonary nodules. No mediastinal adenopathy. No adrenal lesions. No bone lesions detected on soft tissue windows. Subcutaneous nodules on trunk measuring 0.8-1.4 cm at multiple sites. Spleen: normal. No ascites. Impression: Extensive Stage IV metastatic melanoma with hepatic, nodal, and soft tissue involvement.

At age 55 years, the patient had a PET-CT scan (18F-FDG), which showed cancer with metastatic disease. Findings: Hypermetabolic primary site, back (SUVmax 12.4). Right axillary conglomerate nodal mass SUVmax 18.7. Left cervical nodes SUVmax 6.8-9.2. Hepatic lesions: all six lesions FDG-avid, SUVmax 14.3 in largest segment VII lesion. Multiple hypermetabolic subcutaneous nodules on trunk and bilateral upper extremities (at least 9 discrete lesions, SUVmax range 4.1-8.9). No FDG-avid brain lesions. No bone marrow involvement. Impression: Widespread metabolically active metastatic melanoma consistent with M1c (non-CNS) disease. High overall metabolic disease burden.

At age 55 years, the patient had an MRI of the brain with and without contrast (baseline staging), which showed no cancer. No intracranial metastases identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No mass effect. Normal brain parenchyma. Impression: No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease at baseline.

At age 55 years, the patient received first-line palliative-intent targeted therapy with dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily and trametinib 2 mg once daily (BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination) for BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma. Treatment initiated following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion. Rationale: high disease burden with hepatic involvement and elevated LDH, favoring targeted therapy for rapid disease control as first-line approach. Baseline labs: LDH 642 U/L, AST 78 U/L, ALT 54 U/L, total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, LVEF 62% by echocardiogram.

At age 55 years (approximately 6 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with rapid response to therapy. Clinical exam: near-complete resolution of palpable subcutaneous nodules on trunk. Right axillary mass substantially reduced (clinically approximately 50% reduction). Patient reports improved energy and appetite. Performance status improved to ECOG 0. LDH: 214 U/L (markedly decreased from 642 U/L at baseline, trending toward normal). Adverse effects noted: grade 1 fatigue, grade 1 pyrexia (managed with dose interruption x1 day and acetaminophen), grade 1 skin rash (hyperkeratosis palms). No QTc prolongation on ECG. Echocardiogram: LVEF stable at 60%. Plan: continue dabrafenib/trametinib at current doses, next restaging in 10 weeks.

At age 55 years (approximately 3 months after starting targeted therapy), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (first restaging), which showed cancer responding to therapy. RECIST 1.1 response assessment: PARTIAL RESPONSE. Target lesions: right axillary mass decreased from 4.8 cm to 1.9 cm (-60%); hepatic segment VII lesion decreased from 3.1 cm to 1.1 cm (-65%); hepatic segment II lesion decreased from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm (-67%). Non-target lesions: left cervical nodes markedly decreased, subcutaneous nodules largely resolved or below threshold. No new lesions. LDH: 167 U/L (within normal limits). Impression: Excellent partial response to dabrafenib/trametinib, consistent with typical early BRAF inhibitor response in V600E-mutant melanoma.

At age 55 years (approximately 4 months after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient developed a grade 2 cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cuSCC) of the right forearm, a known class-effect adverse event of BRAF inhibitor therapy (paradoxical MAPK activation in RAS-mutant keratinocytes). Managed with wide local excision under local anesthesia. Pathology confirmed well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, margins clear. No dose modification of dabrafenib required per protocol. Dermatology co-management initiated with monthly skin surveillance. Additionally, patient had ongoing grade 1-2 fatigue and intermittent grade 1 pyrexia managed symptomatically.

At age 55 years (approximately 6 months after starting targeted therapy), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (second restaging), which showed cancer with continued partial response but possible early stabilization. Target lesions: right axillary mass 1.7 cm (stable from prior 1.9 cm); hepatic segment VII lesion 1.0 cm (stable); hepatic segment II lesion 0.5 cm (stable). Non-target lesions: stable. No new lesions. RECIST 1.1: stable disease (from nadir, sum of target lesions 3.2 cm vs 3.5 cm from 3-month scan). LDH: 182 U/L (normal). Impression: Disease remains controlled on dabrafenib/trametinib, now approximately at nadir with stable residual disease. No evidence of progression. Continue current therapy with close interval surveillance.

At age 55-56 years (approximately 8 months after starting targeted therapy), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (third restaging), which showed cancer with progressive disease. RECIST 1.1: PROGRESSIVE DISEASE. Target lesions: right axillary mass increased from 1.7 cm to 3.4 cm (+100%); hepatic segment VII lesion increased from 1.0 cm to 2.8 cm (+180%); hepatic segment II lesion increased from 0.5 cm to 1.6 cm (+220%). New lesions: two new hepatic lesions in segment IV (1.3 cm) and segment VI (0.9 cm); multiple new subcutaneous nodules on back and abdomen (largest 1.7 cm); new right external iliac lymph node 1.2 cm. LDH: 589 U/L (markedly elevated, nearly 2x ULN again). Impression: Clear radiographic progression on BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy at 8 months, consistent with acquired resistance. New hepatic and nodal lesions. Urgent oncology review recommended.

At age 56 years (at time of confirmed progression on dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had an urgent oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with progressive disease. Discussion of progression: explained to patient that acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibitors occurs in majority of patients (median PFS approximately 9-12 months), driven by mechanisms including BRAF amplification, MEK1/2 mutations, NRAS mutations, or alternative pathway activation. Re-biopsy of new lesion discussed. Performance status: ECOG 1 (some fatigue, abdominal discomfort from hepatic involvement). Plan: discontinue dabrafenib/trametinib. Recommend re-biopsy of new subcutaneous nodule on abdomen to assess resistance mechanisms. Recommend transition to ipilimumab/nivolumab combination immunotherapy as second-line treatment. Tumor board review completed; recommendation endorsed. Brain MRI ordered to confirm no CNS progression prior to immunotherapy initiation. Informed consent obtained for immunotherapy.

At age 56 years, the patient had a pathology result from core needle biopsy of new subcutaneous nodule on the right abdomen (resistance biopsy, obtained at time of progression on BRAF/MEK inhibitors). RESULT: Metastatic melanoma, consistent with progression on targeted therapy. Immunohistochemistry: S100 positive, HMB-45 positive, Melan-A positive. BRAF V600E IHC (VE1): positive (retained). Noteworthy: high mitotic activity, Ki-67 approximately 65% (increased from 40% at diagnosis). Stromal changes consistent with prior treatment effect. Sent for repeat molecular profiling to assess resistance mechanisms.

At age 56 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed on the resistance biopsy (subcutaneous nodule, right abdomen, FFPE). Comparison with baseline tumor molecular profile. RETAINED ALTERATIONS: BRAF p.V600E (AF 51.3%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN p.R130* (AF 38.2%), TP53 p.R175H (AF 42.1%), TERT promoter c.-124C>T (AF 54.6%), RAC1 p.P29S (AF 9.8%), CCND1 amplification, MYC amplification. NEW/EMERGENT ALTERATIONS (acquired resistance): BRAF amplification (copy number gain ~8x, consistent with BRAF inhibitor resistance mechanism); MAP2K1 p.P124S missense mutation (MEK1 mutation, allele frequency 18.6%, known acquired resistance mutation to MEK inhibitors - reduces drug binding); PIK3CA p.E545K missense mutation (allele frequency 22.4%, emergent, PI3K pathway hyperactivation). TMB: 21.1 mutations/megabase (slightly increased). MSS status retained. Impression: Acquired BRAF inhibitor/MEK inhibitor resistance via BRAF amplification and MEK1 p.P124S mutation; concurrent PI3K pathway activation via PIK3CA mutation. No actionable targets beyond existing resistance landscape. Supports transition to immunotherapy.

At age 56 years (prior to starting second-line immunotherapy), the patient had an MRI of the brain with and without contrast, which showed no cancer. No intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal study. Impression: No CNS involvement. Cleared for ipilimumab/nivolumab immunotherapy.

At age 56 years, the patient received second-line palliative-intent combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks and nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 cycles (nivolumab/ipilimumab combination, CheckMate 067 dosing regimen), followed by planned nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance. Indication: BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma with acquired resistance to dabrafenib/trametinib. Baseline labs prior to immunotherapy: LDH 589 U/L, AST 94 U/L, ALT 78 U/L (mildly elevated, likely from hepatic metastases), total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL, TSH 2.1 mIU/L, ACTH/AM cortisol within normal limits, CBC within normal limits. Thyroid, adrenal, and pituitary function baselines obtained. Autoimmune history: none. Colonoscopy not performed at baseline (no indication).

At age 56 years (after cycle 2 of ipilimumab/nivolumab, approximately 6 weeks into combination immunotherapy), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer present but early signs of immune activation. Patient tolerated cycles 1 and 2 reasonably well. Adverse effects: grade 1-2 fatigue, grade 1 pruritus, grade 1 maculopapular rash on trunk (managed with topical hydrocortisone). Grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day above baseline) noted, counseled regarding immune-mediated colitis warning signs. LDH: 412 U/L (improved from 589 U/L). Performance status ECOG 1. Clinical exam: mild reduction in palpable subcutaneous nodules. Plan: Proceed with cycles 3 and 4 with close monitoring. Stool diary initiated. Low threshold for GI evaluation if diarrhea worsens.

At age 56 years (approximately 9 weeks after starting ipilimumab/nivolumab, following cycle 3), the patient developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis. Presentation: abrupt onset of 8-10 watery, non-bloody bowel movements per day, severe abdominal cramping, dehydration, fever to 38.6 degrees C. Admitted to hospital. Stool studies (C. difficile, bacterial cultures, ova/parasites) negative. Flexible sigmoidoscopy: diffuse erythema, edema, loss of vascular pattern, multiple erosions and superficial ulcerations from rectum to splenic flexure consistent with immune-mediated colitis (grade 3 per CTCAE v5.0). Biopsies: marked cryptitis, crypt abscesses, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes consistent with immune-mediated colitis, no CMV inclusions on H&E (CMV IHC negative). MANAGEMENT: ipilimumab and nivolumab both held. Methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg/day IV initiated. IV fluids, electrolyte repletion, bowel rest. Infectious disease consultation obtained. Gastroenterology co-management. Grade 3 colitis: ipilimumab permanently discontinued per protocol. Nivolumab hold pending steroid taper and clinical improvement.

At age 56 years (2 weeks after grade 3 colitis admission), the patient had partial response to IV methylprednisolone with improvement in stool frequency to 3-4 loose stools/day. Transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (80 mg/day). Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV x1 dose administered at day 10 of steroid therapy for incomplete steroid response (per NCCN immune-mediated colitis management guidelines). Following infliximab, significant clinical improvement: stool frequency reduced to 1-2 formed stools/day within 5 days. Prednisone taper initiated: 10 mg decrease every 2 weeks. GI symptoms fully resolved after 8 weeks total on steroid taper. Colonoscopy performed at 10 weeks post-colitis onset: mucosal healing, no active inflammation, residual mild vascular pattern distortion. Nivolumab cleared to resume after steroid dose reduced to prednisone 10 mg/day and GI symptoms resolved for minimum 2 weeks. Note: ipilimumab permanently discontinued due to grade 3 toxicity; patient to continue nivolumab monotherapy maintenance only.

At age 56 years (approximately 4 months after initial immunotherapy start, following resolution of grade 3 colitis and completion of steroid taper), the patient resumed nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as single-agent maintenance immunotherapy. Decision to resume based on: clinical benefit observed (LDH normalization, partial radiographic response noted on interim imaging), absence of active colitis, prednisone tapered to 5 mg/day (continued as physiologic replacement and slow taper). Gastroenterology clearance obtained. Patient counseled extensively on colitis recurrence risk, warning signs, and strict dietary guidance. Proton pump inhibitor prophylaxis continued.

At age 56 years (approximately 5 months after starting immunotherapy, first restaging on immunotherapy), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer with partial response to immunotherapy. RECIST 1.1 assessment: PARTIAL RESPONSE. Compared to pre-immunotherapy baseline: right axillary mass decreased from 3.4 cm to 1.6 cm (-53%); hepatic segment VII lesion decreased from 2.8 cm to 1.2 cm (-57%); hepatic segment II lesion decreased from 1.6 cm to 0.7 cm (-56%). New hepatic lesions (segment IV 1.3 cm and segment VI 0.9 cm from prior scan): markedly decreased, segment IV lesion 0.4 cm, segment VI lesion not measurable (resolved or below threshold). Subcutaneous nodules: significant reduction, most no longer measurable. Right external iliac node: decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.6 cm. No new lesions. LDH: 198 U/L (near normal). Impression: Significant partial response to immunotherapy despite ipilimumab discontinuation. Ongoing benefit from nivolumab monotherapy.

At age 56 years (approximately 6 months after starting immunotherapy, on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with response to therapy. Patient doing well on nivolumab monotherapy. Performance status ECOG 0-1. No recurrence of GI symptoms on prednisone taper (now 2.5 mg/day). Grade 1 fatigue ongoing. TSH mildly elevated at 5.8 mIU/L (free T4 normal) - consistent with subclinical hypothyroidism, likely immune-mediated; levothyroxine 25 mcg/day initiated. Endocrinology consultation placed. Clinical exam: subcutaneous nodules not palpable. Right axillary region: small residual fullness but no discrete mass. LDH 198 U/L. CBC: mild grade 1 anemia (hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL), likely multifactorial (chronic inflammation, prior therapy). Plan: continue nivolumab 480 mg q4 weeks. Next imaging restaging in 3 months. Thyroid monitoring q6-8 weeks.

At age 56-57 years (approximately 8 months after starting immunotherapy, on nivolumab maintenance), the patient developed grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH 14.2 mIU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL, free T3 low). Symptoms: worsening fatigue, constipation, mild weight gain (4 kg over 2 months), cold intolerance, mild cognitive slowing. Anti-TPO antibodies positive (titer 340 IU/mL), anti-thyroglobulin antibodies positive - consistent with immune-mediated thyroiditis. No thyrotoxic phase identified retrospectively. MANAGEMENT: Levothyroxine dose uptitrated to 75 mcg/day. No corticosteroids required for grade 2 hypothyroidism per guidelines. Nivolumab continued without modification. Endocrinology follow-up. TSH rechecked at 6 weeks: 3.2 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg (adequate replacement). Symptoms resolved over 6-8 weeks. Likely permanent, will require lifelong levothyroxine replacement.

At age 57 years (approximately 9 months after starting immunotherapy, second restaging on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, which showed cancer with continued partial response and deepening response to immunotherapy. RECIST 1.1: PARTIAL RESPONSE (deepening from prior). Target lesions: right axillary mass decreased from 1.6 cm to 0.9 cm (-44%); hepatic segment VII lesion decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.6 cm (-50%); hepatic segment II lesion 0.4 cm (decreased from 0.7 cm). Total target lesion sum: 1.9 cm (baseline pre-immunotherapy sum was 8.3 cm, overall 77% reduction). Segment IV hepatic lesion: not measurable, resolved. No new lesions identified. Subcutaneous nodules: complete resolution. Right external iliac node: 0.4 cm (normal by size). LDH: 152 U/L (within normal limits). Impression: Durable deepening partial response on nivolumab maintenance. No evidence of progression. Excellent treatment response.

At age 57 years (approximately 9 months into immunotherapy), the patient had an MRI of the brain with and without contrast (surveillance), which showed no cancer. No intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal study. Impression: No CNS involvement. Brain MRI surveillance every 6 months while on active treatment per institutional protocol.

At age 57 years (approximately 10 months after starting immunotherapy, on nivolumab maintenance cycle 10), the patient had an oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with continued response to therapy (durable partial response). Patient tolerating nivolumab monotherapy well. No recurrence of colitis. Hypothyroidism well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg/day (TSH 2.8 mIU/L). ECOG performance status: 0. Fatigue significantly improved. Patient returned to modified work schedule (landscaping, light duty). Clinical examination: no palpable lymphadenopathy, no subcutaneous nodules. LDH: 148 U/L. CBC: hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL (improved). LFTs: AST 28 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL (all normal). Assessment: Durable partial response on nivolumab monotherapy with excellent quality of life. Plan: Continue nivolumab 480 mg q4 weeks. Consider PET-CT for more complete metabolic response assessment. Ongoing thyroid monitoring. GI monitoring (no colitis symptoms). Dermatology surveillance for additional cuSCC or other skin malignancies. Repeat brain MRI in 6 months.

At age 57 years (approximately 12 months after starting immunotherapy), the patient had a PET-CT scan (18F-FDG), which showed markedly reduced cancer burden with near-complete metabolic response. Findings: Prior hypermetabolic right axillary mass: small residual soft tissue 0.8 cm, SUVmax 2.1 (markedly decreased from baseline SUVmax 18.7, near background activity). Prior hepatic lesions: no FDG-avid lesions in liver. Segment VII region: faint low-level activity SUVmax 1.8 (below threshold for metabolically active disease). No other FDG-avid lesions. No new hypermetabolic foci anywhere. Background physiologic FDG uptake only. Impression: Near-complete metabolic response (Deauville-equivalent score 2) to immunotherapy in metastatic BRAF V600E melanoma. Residual small right axillary soft tissue most likely represents fibrotic/necrotic residual without active disease. Excellent overall treatment response. Oncology team discussion: treatment holiday considered but deferred given M1c presentation; plan to continue nivolumab maintenance with surveillance imaging every 4 months.

At age 57 years (approximately 14 months after starting immunotherapy, ongoing nivolumab maintenance), the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board discussion and oncologist office assessment, which showed cancer with near-complete response to therapy. Tumor board recommendation: continue nivolumab 480 mg IV q4 weeks given M1c disease, initially high-volume hepatic and nodal burden, and durable benefit without significant ongoing toxicity. Patient agrees with plan to continue indefinitely. Quality of life assessed as excellent (FACT-M score improved significantly from baseline). Ongoing management: levothyroxine 75 mcg/day for immune-mediated hypothyroidism (stable, well-controlled). Surveillance colonoscopy deferred (patient asymptomatic, prior colitis fully healed). Annual dermatology exam. LDH 144 U/L. ECOG 0. Discussion of long-term outcomes: approximately 20% of ipilimumab/nivolumab-treated patients achieve durable long-term remission; patient is tracking toward favorable outcome. Continued close monitoring with imaging every 4 months, brain MRI every 6 months, and thyroid function every 3 months.

documents.text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION — SECOND INTERVAL STUDY ON TARGETED THERAPY

---

SCAN TYPE AND TECHNIQUE
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of iodinated intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL), using a portal venous phase acquisition for abdominal and pelvic imaging and a standard thoracic protocol. Multiplanar reconstructions in the axial, coronal, and sagittal planes were generated and reviewed. This examination is performed for interval restaging in the setting of known metastatic melanoma (BRAF V600E-mutant, Stage IV M1c) on ongoing dabrafenib and trametinib combination targeted therapy. Comparison is made to the prior CT examination performed at approximately 3 months following treatment initiation, and to the original pre-treatment staging CT.

---

FINDINGS

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lung parenchyma is well aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules are identified. No new consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion. The airways are patent to the segmental level. No endobronchial lesions.

Mediastinum: The mediastinal contour is unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes are seen. The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. No anterior mediastinal mass.

Pleura: No pleural effusion. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Chest wall and axillary regions: The previously described right axillary conglomerate nodal mass, which measured 4.8 x 3.9 cm at baseline and decreased to 1.9 cm on the 3-month restaging examination, now measures approximately 1.7 cm in its largest axial dimension on the current study. There is a small central area of residual heterogeneity within this node, but the overall morphology has stabilized compared to the 3-month scan. No new axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No new chest wall soft tissue nodules identified.

Bones (thoracic): No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified on soft tissue or bone windows. No pathological fracture.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. The previously identified hepatic lesions are again seen and assessed for interval change:

  — Segment VII lesion: This hypodense, hypoenhancing lesion now measures approximately 1.0 cm in greatest axial dimension, compared to 1.1 cm on the prior 3-month scan and 3.1 cm at baseline. The lesion has a small, slightly irregular low-attenuation center and minimal peripheral rim enhancement. Overall size is essentially unchanged from the preceding examination, consistent with a plateau in response at or near nadir.

  — Segment II lesion: This lesion measures approximately 0.5 cm, compared to 0.6 cm on the prior examination and 1.8 cm at baseline. The lesion is near the lower limit of reliable measurement. No significant interval change.

  — Additional smaller hepatic foci: Several additional previously noted hypodense foci have remained below the threshold of reliable measurement and continue to be not separately measurable. No new hypodense hepatic lesions are identified.

Bile ducts: No intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. No biliary stones.

Gallbladder: Normal.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in enhancement. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Normal enhancement pattern throughout. No ductal dilatation. No pancreatic mass.

Adrenal glands: Both adrenal glands appear normal in size and attenuation. No adrenal nodules or masses. No interval change.

Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric contrast enhancement and excretion. No focal renal masses. No hydronephrosis.

Bowel: The stomach, small bowel, and visualized large bowel are normal in caliber and wall thickness. No obstruction. No free air. No mesenteric stranding or bowel wall thickening to suggest colitis or other inflammatory process.

Ascites and peritoneum: No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental caking.

Abdominal aorta and vasculature: Normal caliber. No aneurysm. Major mesenteric vessels patent.

Retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes identified.

Abdominal wall and soft tissues: No focal abdominal wall lesions. The previously described cutaneous and subcutaneous satellite nodules over the trunk, which were largely resolved on the 3-month restaging scan, remain below the threshold of reliable measurement or are not visualized. No new subcutaneous nodules identified within the imaged field.

PELVIS:

Urinary bladder: Normal, without wall thickening or intraluminal lesion.

Rectum and sigmoid: Normal wall thickness and caliber.

Pelvic lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged inguinal, obturator, or iliac lymph nodes. No new pelvic nodal disease.

Pelvic bones: No osseous lesions. No focal marrow replacement abnormality.

Soft tissues: No pelvic soft tissue masses.

---

CERVICAL REGION (partially imaged on scout):
The left cervical lymph nodes, previously enlarged at baseline (up to 2.2 cm) and markedly decreased on the 3-month study, are not well visualized on this dedicated chest/abdomen/pelvis examination. Per prior assessment, these were substantially diminished. No new cervical or supraclavicular adenopathy is appreciated on the included scout images.

---

IMPRESSION

1. Known metastatic melanoma (BRAF V600E-mutant) on dabrafenib/trametinib combination targeted therapy, now at approximately 6 months of treatment — overall disease burden continues to appear well controlled with no radiographically evident progression.

2. Right axillary nodal mass: marginally smaller at 1.7 cm compared to 1.9 cm on the 3-month scan, representing a plateau in regression rather than continued interval shrinkage. The lesion has decreased substantially from the pre-treatment baseline of 4.8 cm. Appearance is most consistent with residual treated disease at or approaching treatment nadir.

3. Hepatic metastases: the segment VII lesion is essentially stable at 1.0 cm (3-month: 1.1 cm; baseline: 3.1 cm), and the segment II lesion is stable at approximately 0.5 cm (3-month: 0.6 cm; baseline: 1.8 cm). No new hepatic lesions. The pattern is consistent with disease stabilization after a period of substantial initial regression — a recognized feature of targeted BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy in metastatic melanoma, reflecting treatment at or near nadir.

4. No new sites of metastatic disease identified. No pulmonary nodules, no new nodal disease, no new soft tissue or subcutaneous deposits, no osseous metastases on soft tissue windows.

5. No imaging features to suggest hepatotoxicity or other organ-specific treatment-related adverse changes within the abdomen or chest.

Overall, this examination demonstrates continued disease control on BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy with stable residual target lesions and no interval progression. The disease appears to have reached a plateau of response after the earlier interval of robust regression.

CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
ONCOLOGY RESTAGING — THIRD INTERVAL STUDY

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old male with known Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, primary site skin of the upper back. Patient has been on dabra 150 mg twice daily and tram 2 mg once daily since initial diagnosis. Prior restaging studies demonstrated excellent partial response at three months and disease stabilization at six months. Current study obtained for third interval restaging at approximately eight months of targeted therapy to evaluate for continued disease control versus progression.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following uneventful administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast via right antecubital vein, with images acquired in the portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations generated. Comparison made to prior CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast obtained approximately two months prior (six-month restaging study), and to the original baseline staging CT.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear. No new pulmonary nodules identified. No consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. The airways are patent to the segmental level. No endobronchial lesions. Previously noted absence of pulmonary nodular disease is maintained.

Mediastinum and hila: The heart is normal in size. The pericardium is unremarkable without effusion or thickening. Mediastinal contours are within normal limits. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy by size criteria. The great vessels are unremarkable. No anterior or posterior mediastinal masses.

Chest wall and pleura: No pleural thickening or effusion. No chest wall or rib lesions. The sternum is intact.

Axillae: The right axillary region demonstrates a markedly enlarged and heterogeneous conglomerate nodal mass, now measuring approximately 3.4 x 2.8 cm in the axial plane. This represents a substantial and worrisome increase compared to the prior six-month restaging study, at which time this mass measured 1.7 cm in its longest dimension. Initial response was gratifying and consistent with what one expects from this regimen in BRAF V600E-mutant disease. Mr. Ellison reports he has noticed over the past 3-4 weeks that some of the bumps on his back and abdomen have reappeared and seem to be enlarging. **ASSESSMENT**

Mr. Ellison is a 56-year-old male with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV M1c melanoma with unambiguous disease progression on first-line dabra/Mekinist combination targeted therapy after achieving an initial excellent partial response lasting approximately 6-7 months. His clinical trajectory is unfortunately typical of what we see with BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations -- most patients derive meaningful benefit, often with dramatic early tumor shrinkage, but acquired resistance emerges in the majority, typically within 6-12 months.

I spent considerable time today with Mr. Ellison and his wife explaining the biology of acquired BRAF inhibitor resistance. Resistance mechanisms are varied but frequently include BRAF gene amplification (which effectively overwhelms the drug's inhibitory capacity), acquisition of MEK1/2 mutations that impair drug binding to the kinase domain, NRAS mutations that reactivate the MAPK pathway upstream of BRAF, or activation of parallel survival pathways such as PI3K/AKT signaling. Importantly, the tumor has typically retained its BRAF V600E mutation, but these additional acquired changes render the BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination ineffective. I have requested a re-biopsy of one of the new subcutaneous nodules on the abdomen to confirm the histological diagnosis (not in doubt clinically, but appropriate for tissue banking and resistance profiling), assess Ki-67 and mitotic activity, and send for repeat molecular profiling to characterize the specific acquired resistance mechanism. Understanding the molecular landscape at progression may also inform whether there is any actionable target beyond what is already known, though in the current setting, the most appropriate next step remains immunotherapy.

His LFTs are mildly elevated (AST 94, ALT 78), which likely reflects hepatic tumor burden rather than drug toxicity at this point (dabra and Mekinist are being held as of today). His bilirubin remains normal and albumin is preserved, so he is not in hepatic decompensation. I will recheck LFTs in 2 weeks and prior to initiating immunotherapy.

The most appropriate second-line treatment for BRAF-mutant melanoma that has progressed on targeted therapy is combination checkpoint immunotherapy. The CheckMate 067 regimen -- ipi (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg plus Opdivo (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivo maintenance -- remains the standard of care and is what I am recommending. PD-L1 expression was assessed at diagnosis (TPS 25%, CPS 38 by 28-8 assay), and TMB was intermediate-high at 18.4 mutations/megabase, both reasonably favorable prognostic features for immunotherapy. Notably, the tumor was MSS, which is the rule for melanoma; this does not detract from immunotherapy benefit in melanoma the way it does in colorectal cancer.

Before initiating immunotherapy, I have ordered an MRI of the brain to rule out interval intracranial metastatic disease, as the approach to CNS lesions may change immediate management. I also ordered baseline endocrine labs (AM cortisol, ACTH stimulation test, TSH/free T4, testosterone/gonadotropins) per our pre-immunotherapy baseline protocol. CBC, CMP, and LFTs are being repeated today to establish a pre-treatment laboratory baseline for immune-related adverse event surveillance.

I discussed with Mr. Ellison and his wife in detail the expected side effect profile of ipi plus Opdivo. The combination carries a substantially higher rate of immune-mediated adverse events than either agent alone. Approximately 50-60% of patients will experience some immune-mediated toxicity; grade 3-4 events occur in roughly 55-60%. The most clinically significant include immune-mediated colitis (diarrhea management counseling provided today with a written symptom diary), hepatitis (LFT surveillance), pneumonitis (dyspnea and cough monitoring), endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, hypophysitis, adrenal insufficiency), and dermatitis. I provided the patient and his wife with the clinic's immune toxicity management guide, emergency contact numbers, and clear instructions to present to the emergency department or call our on-call line if he develops diarrhea exceeding 4-6 loose stools per day above baseline, fever, severe abdominal pain, shortness of breath, or any other concerning new symptoms.

I reassured him that the intent of treatment remains to achieve meaningful tumor control and ideally a durable response. We discussed the data from CheckMate 067 showing that approximately 40-50% of patients derive long-term benefit from ipi/Opdivo combination, and that responses in melanoma can be deep and durable. His current performance status (ECOG 1), preserved organ function, and prior demonstrated immune response potential make him a reasonable candidate.

**PLAN**

1. **Discontinue dabra and tram effective today.** Both agents are being stopped given unequivocal disease progression. Drug holiday washout period of approximately 2 weeks prior to immunotherapy initiation to minimize risk of additive toxicities.

   - Standard H&E and melanoma immunohistochemistry panel (S100, HMB-45, Melan-A, SOX10)
   - Ki-67 proliferation index
   - BRAF V600E IHC (VE1) to confirm retention
   - Comprehensive NGS panel (523-gene solid tumor panel, same platform as baseline) to characterize acquired resistance mechanism
   - Tissue banking for future research use (consent obtained)

3. **Brain MRI with and without gadolinium** -- ordered today, results to be reviewed prior to immunotherapy initiation. If CNS metastases identified, neurosurgery and/or radiation oncology consultation will be obtained urgently.

4. **Pre-immunotherapy baseline workup:**
   - Labs: CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs, LDH, TSH/free T4, AM cortisol, ACTH stimulation test, testosterone (in male patients), FSH/LH, Hgb A1c, urinalysis
   - ECG (baseline)
   - LVEF assessment by echocardiogram (given prior echo at baseline of 62% and prior targeted therapy; now establishing new baseline prior to immunotherapy)
   - Hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis B core antibody, hepatitis C antibody (immunotherapy clearance per institutional protocol)

5. **Second-line systemic therapy -- Yervoy (Yervoy) + nivo (Opdivo):** Plan to initiate once brain MRI reviewed and confirmed negative for CNS disease, resistance biopsy obtained, and pre-treatment labs verified. Target start: approximately 2 weeks from today. Dosing: Yervoy 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles in combination with nivo 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles (CheckMate 067 dosing regimen), followed by nivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as single-agent maintenance. Tumor board review of this plan to be confirmed via e-consult prior to first infusion.

6. **Supportive care and monitoring:**
   - Patient education materials regarding immune-mediated adverse events provided and reviewed in detail
   - Stool diary to be maintained starting today

7. **Follow-up:**
   - Clinical appointment in 2 weeks (or sooner if new symptoms) to review brain MRI result, re-biopsy result (if available), baseline labs, and confirm immunotherapy start date
   - First infusion appointment to be scheduled by treatment coordination team

COMMENT:

The morphologic and immunophenotypic features of this right abdominal subcutaneous core needle biopsy are consistent with metastatic melanoma, concordant with the patient's known diagnosis of BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV melanoma, primary site skin of back. The current biopsy shows high-grade histologic features including brisk mitotic activity (estimated >10/mm2), areas of geographic necrosis, and a markedly elevated Ki-67 proliferative index of approximately 60–65%, representing a notable increase in proliferative activity compared to the diagnostic specimen obtained at initial staging (right axillary lymph node core biopsy; Ki-67 approximately 40% at that time). This increase in mitotic rate and proliferative index is consistent with clonal evolution and biologic acceleration in the setting of acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy, as has been well-described in the literature.

Regressed/non-brisk tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and treatment-associated stromal fibrosis are present, consistent with prior exposure to targeted therapy. Retained VE1 (BRAF V600E) immunoreactivity indicates that the BRAF V600E oncoprotein is preserved in the resistant clone; the resistance mechanism is therefore not attributable to loss of the target mutation, and molecular profiling (result pending) is necessary to characterize the specific resistance mechanism (e.g., BRAF amplification, BRAF splice variant, MEK1/2 mutation, NRAS mutation, or alternative pathway activation).

This material has been forwarded for somatic NGS molecular profiling as noted above. Correlation with clinical history, prior molecular data, and pending molecular results is recommended.

Comparison with prior material: Prior right axillary lymph node core biopsy (SP-[REDACTED]) demonstrated metastatic melanoma with Ki-67 ~40%, mitotic rate 6/mm2, S100+/HMB-45+/Melan-A+/SOX10+/BRAF VE1+, and PD-L1 TPS 25%/CPS 38. The current biopsy is morphologically consistent with the prior diagnosis, with evidence of histologic progression.

CLINICAL CONTEXT AND INDICATION
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Mr. Ellison is a 56-year-old male with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV metastatic melanoma (T4bN3M1c) who was initiated on first-line targeted therapy with dabra 150 mg twice daily and Mekinist 2 mg once daily approximately 8 months prior to this biopsy. He achieved a robust initial partial response (RECIST 1.1 partial response at 3-month restaging, with up to 67% regression of target lesions), followed by disease stabilization. However, third restaging CT at approximately 8 months demonstrated unequivocal progressive disease: right axillary mass increased from 1.7 cm to 3.4 cm, hepatic segment VII lesion from 1.0 cm to 2.8 cm, hepatic segment II lesion from 0.5 cm to 1.6 cm, and emergence of new hepatic lesions and subcutaneous nodules. Serum LDH rose to 589 U/L (nearly 2x ULN). A core needle biopsy of a new subcutaneous nodule on the right abdomen was performed to characterize the molecular basis of acquired resistance and inform subsequent therapeutic strategy. This report details the integrated analysis comparing the resistance biopsy molecular profile with the diagnostic baseline NGS performed on the right axillary lymph node metastasis.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY OF FINDINGS
------------------------------------------------------------------------
RESULT INTERPRETATION: RESISTANCE PROFILE WITH MULTIPLE ACQUIRED MECHANISMS

This tumor demonstrates acquired resistance to combined BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy via at least two independent, convergent resistance mechanisms: (1) high-level BRAF amplification (copy number gain ~8x), which restores ERK phosphorylation and overcomes BRAF inhibitor blockade even in the setting of continued drug exposure, and (2) an emergent MAP2K1 p.P124S missense mutation (MEK1), a well-characterized allosteric resistance mutation that reduces tram binding affinity and restores downstream MAPK signaling through the MEK-ERK axis. Additionally, a newly acquired PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation was identified, representing emergent PI3K pathway activation that may provide an alternative pro-survival signal independent of the MAPK pathway. These findings are consistent with the well-described biology of acquired BRAF/MEK inhibitor resistance in melanoma and support transition to alternative therapeutic strategy.

GENOMIC ALTERATIONS - SOMATIC FINDINGS
------------------------------------------------------------------------

--- RETAINED ALTERATIONS (present in baseline and resistance biopsy) ---

1. BRAF p.V600E (c.1799T>A) - Retained
   Gene Function: Serine-threonine kinase; MAPK pathway driver
   Variant Classification: Oncogenic missense mutation
   Allele Frequency: 51.3% (baseline: 48.2%)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic (Tier I, Level A)
   Clinical Significance: Founding oncogenic driver, retained at progression. Confirms persistence of BRAF-driven biology in resistant clone. The maintained or slightly increased allele frequency is consistent with tumor clonal evolution retaining the driver event. Note: although the BRAF inhibitor binding site (p.V600) is retained, resistance is mediated through copy number amplification (see below) and downstream pathway reactivation rather than mutation of the drug-binding residue itself.

2. CDKN2A - Homozygous Deletion (Retained)
   Gene Function: Tumor suppressor; encodes p16INK4A (CDK4/6 inhibitor) and p14ARF (MDM2 inhibitor/p53 stabilizer)
   Copy Number: Complete homozygous deletion (chr9p21.3)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic (Tier I)
   Clinical Significance: Loss of both p16INK4A and p14ARF functions, resulting in constitutive CDK4/6-mediated cell cycle progression and impaired p53 stabilization. This deletion was present at baseline and is retained in the resistant clone, indicating it is a truncal alteration in this tumor. CDKN2A loss is one of the most common alterations in melanoma and is associated with aggressive biology.

3. PTEN p.R130* (c.388C>T) - Retained
   Gene Function: Lipid phosphatase; PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway negative regulator
   Variant Classification: Nonsense mutation (loss of function)
   Allele Frequency: 38.2% (baseline: 41.1%; within expected variation)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic / Loss of Function (Tier I)
   Clinical Significance: Truncating mutation at a well-characterized hotspot (codon 130 in the phosphatase domain). Results in constitutive PI3K/AKT pathway activation. Retained as a clonal event. Of note, the emergence of PIK3CA p.E545K at this time point (see novel findings below) may compound PI3K pathway hyperactivation in the resistant clone, potentially representing synthetic convergence on PI3K signaling as a bypass survival mechanism.

4. TP53 p.R175H (c.524G>A) - Retained
   Gene Function: Transcription factor; master tumor suppressor ("guardian of the genome")
   Variant Classification: Gain-of-function missense mutation
   Allele Frequency: 42.1% (baseline: 39.8%)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic; Gain of Function (Tier I)
   Clinical Significance: R175H is among the most common and best-characterized gain-of-function TP53 mutations in human cancer. This substitution abrogates wild-type tumor suppressor activity and confers neomorphic oncogenic functions including enhanced invasion, genomic instability, and resistance to apoptosis. Retained as a clonal truncal alteration. Gain-of-function TP53 mutations have been associated with resistance to multiple therapies and may contribute to the aggressive resistance phenotype observed here.

5. TERT Promoter c.-124C>T - Retained
   Gene Region: TERT promoter region (chr5p15.33, position -124 upstream of ATG)
   Variant Classification: Promoter mutation (creates de novo ETS/TCF binding site)
   Allele Frequency: 54.6% (baseline: 52.3%)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic (Tier I)
   Clinical Significance: This is the most common TERT promoter hotspot mutation (C228T), present in approximately 70-75% of cutaneous melanomas. Creates a de novo binding site for ETS transcription factors, leading to aberrant upregulation of telomerase reverse transcriptase, telomere maintenance, and replicative immortality. The high allele frequency (~54%) suggests this is a very early (possibly truncal) and clonally dominant event. Associated with poor prognosis in melanoma independent of other factors.

6. RAC1 p.P29S (c.85C>T) - Retained
   Gene Function: Small GTPase; cytoskeletal dynamics, cell migration, survival signaling
   Variant Classification: Oncogenic missense mutation
   Allele Frequency: 9.8% (baseline: 11.4%; within range of sampling variation)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic (Tier II)
   Clinical Significance: RAC1 p.P29S is a recurrent UV-signature mutation found in approximately 5-10% of cutaneous melanomas. This mutation renders RAC1 constitutively GTP-bound and promotes cell proliferation, invasion, and metastasis. The relatively low and slightly decreased allele frequency compared to baseline is consistent with its presence as a subclonal variant. The UV-induced nature of this mutation (C>T at dipyrimidine context, SBS7 signature) further supports the UV-driven mutagenesis underlying this tumor's origin.

7. CCND1 Amplification - Retained
   Gene Function: Cyclin D1; CDK4/6 activator, G1/S transition
   Copy Number: High-level amplification (chr11q13, copy number ~11x, estimated 8 additional copies)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic (Tier II)
   Clinical Significance: CCND1 amplification drives constitutive CDK4/6 activation and bypasses the p16INK4A checkpoint (particularly relevant in the context of concurrent CDKN2A deletion, which removes the opposing constraint). Retained as a truncal copy number event. CCND1 amplification has been associated with resistance to multiple targeted therapies in various cancer types.

8. MYC Amplification - Retained
   Gene Function: MYC transcription factor; master regulator of cell growth, biosynthesis, and proliferation
   Copy Number: High-level amplification (chr8q24, copy number ~9x)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic (Tier II)
   Clinical Significance: MYC amplification is present in approximately 15-20% of advanced melanomas and confers proliferative advantage. Retained as a truncal event. MYC amplification has been implicated in resistance to apoptosis and may contribute to the aggressive progression phenotype seen here.

--- NOVEL / EMERGENT ALTERATIONS (acquired at resistance, not detected at baseline) ---

9. BRAF Amplification - NEWLY ACQUIRED (Primary Resistance Mechanism)
   Gene Function: BRAF serine-threonine kinase; MAPK pathway
   Copy Number: High-level focal amplification (~8x copy number gain, chr7q34)
   Pathogenicity: Oncogenic; Resistance Mechanism (Tier I, Level A)
   Clinical Significance: BRAF amplification is one of the most common mechanisms of acquired resistance to BRAF inhibitor therapy, occurring in approximately 10-20% of resistant melanomas. This mechanism restores ERK signaling by overwhelming the inhibitory capacity of Zelboraf, Tafinlar, and related agents - the massively increased BRAF protein concentration effectively outcompetes drug binding at pharmacologically achievable concentrations. Notably, this resistance mechanism occurs in the context of retained BRAF V600E mutation, meaning the amplified copies are presumed to be the mutant allele (consistent with the observed increase in BRAF V600E allele frequency). This also renders re-challenge with BRAF inhibitors futile at standard dosing. This alteration was ABSENT in the baseline NGS profile from the axillary lymph node metastasis, confirming its acquisition under selective pressure of dabra therapy.

10. MAP2K1 p.P124S (c.370C>T) - NEWLY ACQUIRED (Co-Primary Resistance Mechanism)
    Gene Function: MEK1 (MAPK/ERK kinase 1); MAPK pathway kinase immediately downstream of BRAF/RAS
    Variant Classification: Missense mutation
    Allele Frequency: 18.6%
    Pathogenicity: Oncogenic; Resistance Mechanism (Tier I, Level A)
    Clinical Significance: MAP2K1 (MEK1) mutations are among the most well-characterized acquired resistance mechanisms to MEK inhibitor therapy. The p.P124S substitution is located in the allosteric pocket of MEK1 that is targeted by allosteric MEK inhibitors including Mekinist (GSK1120212). This mutation reduces MEK1 conformational flexibility required for drug binding without substantially affecting kinase catalytic activity, thereby restoring MEK-ERK signaling in the presence of tram. This exact mutation (p.P124S) has been reported in multiple published case series of patients progressing on MEK inhibitor-containing regimens and is considered a functionally validated resistance allele. The subclonal allele frequency (18.6%) suggests this may have arisen as a secondary resistance event or may represent selective outgrowth of a minor pre-existing clone. Together with BRAF amplification, these two alterations represent convergent, possibly independent resistance mechanisms operating in parallel subpopulations within this tumor. This alteration was ABSENT in the baseline NGS profile, confirming acquired nature.

11. PIK3CA p.E545K (c.1633G>A) - NEWLY ACQUIRED (Secondary / Bypass Resistance Pathway)
    Gene Function: PI3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha (PI3Kα); PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway
    Variant Classification: Hotspot activating missense mutation (helical domain)
    Allele Frequency: 22.4%
    Pathogenicity: Oncogenic (Tier I, Level B)
    Clinical Significance: PIK3CA p.E545K is one of the two most common hotspot activating mutations in PIK3CA (along with p.H1047R), and is located in the helical domain of the PI3Kα catalytic subunit. This mutation constitutively activates PI3K, leading to enhanced AKT and mTOR signaling and increased cell survival, proliferation, and resistance to apoptosis. The emergence of this mutation at resistance is clinically significant: in the setting of pre-existing PTEN p.R130* (baseline), this additional PIK3CA gain-of-function mutation represents compounding, dual PI3K pathway activation. This may represent an alternative survival pathway that bypasses MAPK-directed therapy, providing a parallel anti-apoptotic signal independent of BRAF/MEK reactivation. PIK3CA activating mutations have been observed as acquired resistance alterations in BRAF-inhibitor-treated melanoma in published literature, typically emerging at a subclonal frequency consistent with what is observed here. This alteration was ABSENT in the baseline NGS profile, confirming acquired nature. While PI3K pathway inhibitors (alpelisib) exist, their activity in this molecular context remains investigational.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTEGRATED RESISTANCE MECHANISM SUMMARY
------------------------------------------------------------------------
This resistance biopsy demonstrates a genomically complex acquired resistance landscape following 8 months of Tafinlar/Mekinist combination BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy. Two high-confidence, functionally validated resistance mechanisms have been identified:

Mechanism 1 - BRAF Amplification (~8x copy number gain):
Restores ERK pathway signaling by increasing BRAF protein levels beyond the inhibitory threshold of dabra. This is a well-established resistance mechanism; the high copy number gain (~8x) is particularly notable and suggests strong selective pressure. Re-treatment with BRAF inhibitors (dabra, Zelboraf, Braftovi) or standard-dose MEK inhibitor alone would be unlikely to achieve disease control.

Mechanism 2 - MAP2K1 p.P124S (MEK1 Allosteric Resistance Mutation, AF 18.6%):
Reduces tram binding to MEK1 via structural alteration of the allosteric binding pocket, restoring MEK-ERK activity in the presence of tram. This mutation has been functionally validated in multiple published resistance studies. The subclonal frequency suggests this may represent either a secondary resistance event or selective expansion of a pre-existing minor clone.

Mechanism 3 (Secondary / Bypass) - PIK3CA p.E545K (AF 22.4%):
Emergent activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, providing a survival signal parallel to and independent of the MAPK pathway. In the context of pre-existing PTEN p.R130* (baseline), this represents compounding PI3K pathway dysregulation. While not a canonical primary resistance mechanism to BRAF/MEK inhibitors, PI3K pathway activation may contribute to resistance to apoptosis and represent an additional selective advantage for the resistant clone.

PATHWAY REACTIVATION MODEL (Inferred):
RAS (wild-type) --> [BRAF V600E, amplified ~8x] --> [MEK1 p.P124S, reduced tram binding] --> [ERK] --> Proliferation/Survival (MAPK pathway reactivated)
PI3K (PIK3CA p.E545K) --> [PTEN p.R130*, non-functional] --> AKT --> mTOR --> Survival/Anti-apoptosis (PI3K bypass pathway active)

CLINICAL ACTIONABILITY AND THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS
------------------------------------------------------------------------

Targeted Therapy:
- BRAF inhibitor re-challenge (dabra, vem, enco): NOT recommended as monotherapy or standard combination; BRAF amplification and MEK1 p.P124S mutation confer resistance
- Higher-dose or next-generation BRAF inhibitor strategies: Experimental; not standard of care
- MEK inhibitor monotherapy: NOT recommended; MEK1 p.P124S directly mediates MEK inhibitor resistance
- PI3K inhibitors (alpelisib): PIK3CA p.E545K is a targetable alteration; however, PI3K inhibitor activity in melanoma is investigational and no approved indication exists at this time; clinical trial consideration appropriate
- ERK inhibitors (investigational): May retain activity downstream of BRAF amplification and MEK1 resistance mutation; clinical trial recommended if available
- CDK4/6 inhibitors: CDKN2A loss and CCND1 amplification present; CDK4/6 inhibitor activity in melanoma is investigational

Immunotherapy:
- TRANSITION TO CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR THERAPY IS SUPPORTED by this molecular profile:
  * TMB 21.1 mut/Mb (intermediate-high, FDA-defined TMB-High threshold >=10 met)
  * UV-dominant mutational signature (associated with neoantigen generation)
  * Prior PD-L1 TPS 25% / CPS 38 (suggestive of immune-responsive biology)
  * MSS status does not preclude immunotherapy benefit in melanoma (unlike colorectal cancer)
  * No HLA loss or beta-2-microglobulin mutations detected that would predict primary immunotherapy resistance
- Anti-PD-1 monotherapy (Opdivo, Keytruda) or anti-CTLA-4/PD-1 combination (Yervoy + Opdivo) represents the recommended next therapeutic strategy per NCCN guidelines for BRAF-mutant metastatic melanoma after BRAF/MEK inhibitor failure

Clinical Trial Eligibility:
- ERK inhibitor trials (e.g., MK-8353, LY3214996, ulixertinib): Mechanism-based rationale given BRAF amplification with retained V600E and MEK1 resistance mutation
- PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitor combination trials: PIK3CA p.E545K + PTEN loss combination
- Tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy trials: Consideration given BRAF V600E background and high neoantigen load inferred from TMB and UV signature

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
55-year-old male (date of birth 1969) with known Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, primary site right upper back, with prior documented hepatic, nodal, and subcutaneous metastatic involvement. Patient recently confirmed to have radiographic and clinical progression on dabrafenib/trametinib combination targeted therapy after approximately 8 months of treatment. Pre-treatment brain MRI was negative for intracranial disease at time of initial diagnosis. Imaging now requested to reassess for interval development of intracranial metastases prior to planned initiation of second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab and nivolumab. History of elevated LDH (589 U/L at time of progression). Resistance biopsy and molecular profiling recently completed.

COMPARISON:
Prior MRI brain with and without contrast (baseline staging, performed approximately 8 months prior): No intracranial metastatic disease identified at that time.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiparametric MRI of the brain performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1-weighted (pre- and post-gadolinium), axial T1-weighted MPRAGE (pre- and post-gadolinium), axial T2-weighted fast spin echo, axial FLAIR (fluid-attenuated inversion recovery), axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial gradient echo susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), and coronal FLAIR. Intravenous gadolinium-based contrast (gadobutrol, 0.1 mmol/kg) administered without complication. Standard three-plane post-contrast T1-weighted imaging obtained, including thin-slice high-resolution volumetric acquisition through the entire intracranial compartment. Total imaging time approximately 45 minutes. Adequate image quality. No motion artifact degradation.

FINDINGS:

BRAIN PARENCHYMA:
The cerebral hemispheres demonstrate normal gray-white matter differentiation bilaterally without focal signal abnormality on T1, T2, or FLAIR sequences. No cortical or subcortical lesions suspicious for metastatic deposit are identified in either hemisphere. No parenchymal signal change to suggest vasogenic edema, cytotoxic edema, or infiltrative process. Cycle 2 of Ipilimumab/Nivolumab -- Treatment Tolerance and Response Assessment

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Marcus Ellison, a 56-year-old male with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, presents for his scheduled follow-up visit following Cycle 2 of combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab and nivolumab. He is being seen approximately 6 weeks after initiation of his second-line systemic regimen. He reports overall tolerance that is manageable, though not without side effects.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Ellison was initially diagnosed with Stage IV (M1c) metastatic melanoma arising from a 6.1 mm ulcerated nodular melanoma on the upper back, with bilateral axillary and left cervical nodal involvement, multiple hepatic metastases, and widespread subcutaneous satellite nodules. His tumor harbored a BRAF V600E mutation confirmed on comprehensive molecular profiling, and he was started on dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg once daily as first-line therapy given his high hepatic disease burden and markedly elevated LDH at presentation (642 U/L). He had a rapid and gratifying initial response -- at 6 weeks his palpable subcutaneous deposits had largely melted away, and his LDH normalized; restaging CT at 3 months confirmed excellent partial response with the right axillary mass down by roughly 60% and the main liver lesions down by two-thirds. There was a rough plateau through the 6-month scan. Unfortunately, his 8-month CT demonstrated unmistakable progression with near-doubling of the axillary mass, marked enlargement of the hepatic lesions, two new hepatic deposits, new subcutaneous nodules, and a new right external iliac node -- along with LDH back up to 589 U/L. He was counseled extensively about acquired BRAF/MEK inhibitor resistance; repeat biopsy of a new subcutaneous abdominal nodule confirmed metastatic melanoma with acquired BRAF amplification and a MEK1 p.P124S resistance mutation, as well as an emergent PIK3CA E545K mutation on next-generation sequencing.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board review and brain MRI confirming no intracranial involvement, Mr. Ellison was started on second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV and nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks, dosed per the CheckMate 067 regimen with four planned induction cycles to be followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. He received Cycle 1 approximately 6 weeks ago without significant acute infusion-related events. Cycle 2 was administered approximately 3 weeks ago, again without acute reactions. He presents today for routine treatment monitoring and toxicity assessment prior to proceeding with Cycle 3.

Since starting immunotherapy, the patient reports the following:
- Fatigue: present and somewhat bothersome, grade 1-2 in severity. He describes feeling more tired than usual, particularly in the afternoons, but is still able to carry out basic activities of daily living and has been going for short walks. He does not feel incapacitated.
- Skin: Mild itchiness over the trunk and arms, grade 1. He notes a new fine, pinkish maculopapular rash on his anterior chest and upper back that appeared about 2 weeks ago. He has been applying over-the-counter hydrocortisone cream with partial relief. No blistering, no mucosal involvement, no skin sloughing.
- Gastrointestinal: He notes loosening of his stools over the past week -- going from his baseline of one formed bowel movement daily to approximately 2-3 loose (not watery) stools per day. No blood in stool, no mucus, no urgency, no nocturnal diarrhea. No significant abdominal cramping at this time. No nausea or vomiting. He is eating and drinking without difficulty.
- He denies fever or chills.
- He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations.
- He denies headache, visual changes, confusion, focal neurologic symptoms.
- He denies joint pains or myalgias beyond the general fatigue.
- He denies dysuria or hematuria.
- He denies any oral ulcers.

On clinical examination, and comparing to his exam at the time of starting immunotherapy, there appears to be some early reduction in the palpable soft tissue disease. The right axillary region is still palpable but appears somewhat smaller and softer than at baseline. This is an early and encouraging sign, particularly given that immunotherapy-mediated responses tend to lag behind targeted therapy responses and can take 3-4 months to manifest clearly on imaging.

Laboratory results reviewed today are notable for LDH of 412 U/L -- this represents a meaningful decline from the pre-immunotherapy baseline of 589 U/L, consistent with early biologic response. LFTs remain mildly elevated (AST and ALT in the 70-80 range, consistent with residual hepatic disease burden rather than hepatitis; the trend will be followed). CBC is unremarkable.

Overall, the picture is encouraging: early clinical and biochemical evidence of immune activation and early tumor response, in the context of manageable but real immune-related toxicity.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, on second-line combination immunotherapy (ipilimumab/nivolumab, CheckMate 067 dosing), status post Cycle 2 of 4 induction cycles:

The patient is at an early but promising juncture in his immunotherapy course. LDH has dropped by over 30% from the pre-treatment baseline, and clinical examination suggests some early softening and reduction of the right axillary disease. The rash and loose stools are expected early signals of immune activation -- the same immune effectors that produce these toxicities are likely also attacking the tumor. We discussed with Mr. Ellison that immunotherapy responses often take longer to manifest radiographically than targeted therapy responses, and that the first restaging CT will be obtained around the 3-month mark. He is encouraged by his own clinical sense of improvement and the falling LDH.

Plan:
- Proceed with Cycle 3 of ipilimumab/nivolumab on schedule today, pending labs reviewed and toxicity assessment confirming safety to treat. Labs as reviewed above do not reveal any hold criteria (no grade 3+ hepatitis, no significant cytopenias, no electrolyte abnormalities).
- Pre-medicate with antiemetics (ondansetron 8 mg IV) and routine supportive care per nursing protocol.
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast planned for approximately 6 weeks from now (approximately 3 months from cycle 1), with interval brain MRI.

2. Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) -- skin and gastrointestinal:

Grade 1 maculopapular rash: Continue topical hydrocortisone cream (1%) twice daily to affected areas. Patient instructed to avoid hot showers, use fragrance-free moisturizers, and protect from direct sun exposure. No systemic steroids indicated at grade 1. Dermatology referral for baseline full-body skin exam also to cover ongoing monitoring for new cuSCC given prior BRAF inhibitor-related squamous cell carcinoma on the right forearm.

Grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day above baseline): This is below the threshold requiring treatment hold. The patient has been counseled extensively about the signs and symptoms of immune-mediated colitis, which can develop suddenly and escalate. I explained clearly: if stools increase to 4 or more per day above baseline, if there is any blood in the stool, severe cramping, fever, or dehydration, he is to call the on-call oncology nurse immediately and proceed to the emergency department if symptoms are severe. He should not wait for a scheduled appointment.
- Initiated formal stool diary: patient to log daily bowel movement frequency, consistency, and any associated symptoms.
- Loperamide 2 mg orally after each loose stool (maximum 8 mg/day) offered for symptomatic relief; patient agreeable.
- Dietary counseling: low-residue diet encouraged (avoid high-fiber foods, raw vegetables, dairy for now), increase fluid intake.
- Low threshold for urgent GI evaluation and possible sigmoidoscopy/colonoscopy if diarrhea worsens.
- Stool cultures (C. diff PCR, bacterial pathogens, ova and parasites) ordered as baseline in the event of escalation requiring corticosteroid use.
- Current diarrhea does NOT require a treatment hold; will re-assess at cycle 3 visit and at next follow-up.

3. Immune-related hypothyroidism monitoring:

TSH is within normal limits today at 2.8 mIU/L. No intervention required. Will monitor at every other cycle. Patient counseled that immune-mediated thyroid dysfunction can develop at any point during immunotherapy, often presenting with fatigue (which is non-specific), and that routine thyroid monitoring is essential. Signs of hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, weight gain) and hyperthyroidism (palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss) reviewed.

4. Mild anemia (grade 1, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL):

Likely multifactorial: chronic disease, prior cytotoxic insult from prior dabrafenib/trametinib therapy, early immune activation. No reticulocytopenia or hemolytic features on smear review. Will monitor at each cycle. Dietary iron and folate encouraged. No transfusion needed or anticipated at current hemoglobin.

5. Ongoing mild elevation of hepatic transaminases (AST 76, ALT 71 U/L):

These values are stable-to-mildly improving from the immunotherapy baseline (AST 94, ALT 78 at start) and are most consistent with residual hepatic metastatic involvement rather than immune-mediated hepatitis. Grades 1 transaminase elevation does not require treatment hold per guidelines. Will continue to monitor carefully -- the pattern to watch for would be a rising AST/ALT not explained by tumor progression, rising bilirubin, or jaundice, which would raise concern for checkpoint inhibitor-related hepatitis. Current values: no hold criteria met.

6. Prior cuSCC right forearm (managed with excision approximately 4 months ago):

Dermatology surveillance ongoing. No recurrence noted on exam today. Dermatology follow-up appointment confirmed.

7. Performance status and quality of life:

ECOG PS: 1. Patient reports that despite fatigue, he feels notably better than he did in the weeks preceding immunotherapy start, when rapid hepatic and nodal progression was causing abdominal discomfort and significant malaise. He has a positive attitude. Psychosocial: patient coping well; offered social work referral, patient declined at this time but is aware of the resource.

- Next clinic visit: 3 weeks (prior to Cycle 4).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Ellison is a 57-year-old landscaper with a well-characterized disease course now spanning approximately 26 months since initial diagnosis. In brief: he was diagnosed with nodular melanoma of the upper back (Breslow 6.1 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V, mitotic rate 6/mm2) presenting de novo with Stage IV disease including bilateral axillary and left cervical lymphadenopathy, six hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm, segment VII), and multiple cutaneous/subcutaneous satellite nodules. LDH at diagnosis was 642 U/L (approximately 2x ULN). BRAF V600E mutation confirmed by both IHC (VE1 antibody, strongly positive) and next-generation sequencing (allele frequency 48.2% on 523-gene panel). PD-L1 TPS 25% / CPS 38.

First-line treatment was dabra (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus tram (Mekinist) 2 mg daily, chosen in the context of high disease burden and hepatic involvement with elevated LDH, favoring rapid disease control. He achieved an excellent early response — LDH dropped from 642 to 214 U/L by 6 weeks, with marked reduction in palpable disease. Restaging CT at 3 months confirmed robust partial response (right axillary mass 4.8 cm → 1.9 cm, hepatic segment VII 3.1 cm → 1.1 cm). He experienced a grade 2 cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cuSCC) of the right forearm at approximately 4 months — a recognized class-effect BRAF inhibitor adverse event — managed with wide local excision; no dose modification was required. Disease stabilized at the 6-month CT but progressed significantly by the 8-month restaging: right axillary mass grew back to 3.4 cm, the hepatic lesions re-expanded substantially, and new hepatic lesions (segments IV and VI) and a right external iliac node appeared. LDH surged to 589 U/L. Resistance biopsy confirmed retained BRAF V600E with newly emergent BRAF amplification (~8x copy number gain) and MAP2K1 p.P124S (MEK1 resistance mutation), along with a new PIK3CA p.E545K mutation — a pattern consistent with bypass of BRAF/MEK inhibition.

Tafinlar/tram was discontinued. After pre-immunotherapy brain MRI confirmed no CNS disease, he was transitioned to second-line combination immunotherapy: Yervoy (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg plus Opdivo (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 planned cycles (CheckMate 067 dosing). He tolerated cycles 1 and 2 with manageable grade 1-2 toxicities (fatigue, pruritus, maculopapular rash, grade 1 diarrhea). After cycle 3, however, he developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis: 8-10 watery bowel movements per day, severe cramping, dehydration, fever to 38.6°C. He was hospitalized; sigmoidoscopy confirmed diffuse erosive colitis from rectum to splenic flexure; infectious workup including C. diff PCR was negative; biopsies showed marked cryptitis with crypt abscesses and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (CMV IHC negative). He was managed with IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg/day then oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, with infliximab 5 mg/kg x1 for incomplete steroid response at day 10. ipi was permanently discontinued given grade 3 severity. He improved substantially on infliximab and completed an extended steroid taper over approximately 8 weeks. Colonoscopy at 10 weeks post-onset confirmed mucosal healing. nivo was restarted as single-agent maintenance (480 mg q4 weeks) approximately 4 months after immunotherapy initiation, once prednisone was tapered to 5 mg/day and GI symptoms had been absent for at least 2 weeks.

Since resuming nivo monotherapy, he has shown an impressive and deepening treatment response. Restaging CT at approximately 5 months post-immunotherapy-start demonstrated significant partial response across all target lesions (right axillary mass 3.4 cm → 1.6 cm, hepatic segment VII 2.8 cm → 1.2 cm, segment II 1.6 cm → 0.7 cm). LDH normalized to 198 U/L. Follow-up clinical note at approximately 6 months documented ongoing response with no colitis recurrence, though TSH was mildly elevated at 5.8 mIU/L (subclinical hypothyroidism); levothyroxine 25 mcg/day was initiated and endocrinology was consulted. At approximately 8 months on immunotherapy, he developed overt grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH 14.2 mIU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL) with symptoms of fatigue, constipation, weight gain, cold intolerance, and mild cognitive slowing; anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies were positive. Levothyroxine was uptitrated to 75 mcg/day; no corticosteroids were required. Repeat TSH at 6 weeks confirmed adequate replacement at 3.2 mIU/L, and symptoms resolved. Second restaging CT at approximately 9 months showed further deepening of response: right axillary mass 1.6 cm → 0.9 cm, hepatic segment VII 1.2 cm → 0.6 cm, segment II 0.7 cm → 0.4 cm; total target lesion sum reduced approximately 77% from the pre-immunotherapy baseline. No new lesions. Concurrent brain MRI surveillance was negative for CNS disease.

Today's visit is at approximately 10 months after immunotherapy start (cycle 10 of nivo maintenance). Mr. Ellison reports that he is doing extremely well overall. His energy has returned to near-normal. He has resumed a modified work schedule with his landscaping business (light duty). He denies any diarrhea, abdominal cramping, or GI complaints. No rash. No dyspnea. No chest pain or palpitations. He feels that his fatigue, which had been a persistent grade 1 complaint throughout much of his treatment course, has now largely resolved. He reports no fevers, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. He has noted mild and stable constipation, which he attributes in part to his levothyroxine therapy and which is managed with dietary modification and occasional stool softener use. He denies any new skin lesions, and he has been keeping up with his monthly dermatology surveillance visits as recommended. He has not noted any visual changes, headaches, focal neurologic deficits, or signs that would raise concern for CNS involvement.

He takes levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (AM, on empty stomach) for immune-mediated hypothyroidism and prednisone, now tapered to 2.5 mg once daily. He is tolerant of his current regimen and has no new medication concerns.
