ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Marcus Ellison is a 57-year-old male with metastatic BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV nodular melanoma (M1c, non-CNS), now approximately 10 months into immunotherapy (roughly 26 months from initial diagnosis). He is on Opdivo (Opdivo) 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance monotherapy following inability to complete combination Yervoy/nivo due to grade 3 immune-mediated colitis after cycle 3 (ipi permanently discontinued). He is tolerating Opdivo monotherapy well and showing an excellent, durable, deepening response.

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA — NEAR-COMPLETE RESPONSE ON nivo MONOTHERAPY

Mr. Ellison has had a remarkable overall treatment response. After rapid initial response to dabra/Mekinist followed by acquired resistance at 8 months, he transitioned to checkpoint immunotherapy. Despite early complicated by grade 3 colitis limiting Yervoy to 3 cycles, he has achieved deep and ongoing tumor control on Opdivo alone. His disease trajectory — with LDH now at 148 U/L (its lowest recorded point), nearly all target lesions reduced to near-resolution on imaging, and no palpable lymphadenopathy or subcutaneous nodules — is highly encouraging.

Plan:
- Administer nivo 480 mg IV today (cycle 10). Pre-medications per standard protocol. Infusion over 30 minutes.
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months (4-month imaging interval agreed upon given depth of response and stability of residual lesions).
- PET-CT (18F-FDG, whole body) ordered now to fully characterize the metabolic activity of residual soft tissue in the right axilla and any residual hepatic signals. This will help distinguish truly resolved disease from fibrotic/necrotic residual and will inform future treatment duration discussions.
- Brain MRI to be repeated in approximately 2-3 months (6-month interval from last MRI).
- Continue Opdivo indefinitely per tumor board recommendation given M1c disease, initial high volume hepatic involvement, and risk of occult residual disease. Duration of Opdivo therapy will be revisited as data matures; no treatment holiday planned at this time.

2. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM — STABLE, WELL-CONTROLLED

Grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism diagnosed approximately 8 months into immunotherapy, treated with levothyroxine uptitrated to 75 mcg/day. Today's TSH 2.8 mIU/L — excellent control. Free T4 normal. Patient asymptomatic from a thyroid standpoint.

Plan:
- Continue levothyroxine (Synthroid) 75 mcg PO daily.
- Recheck TSH and free T4 in approximately 3 months (thyroid monitoring every 3 months ongoing per protocol).
- Endocrinology aware; follow-up appointment scheduled independently. Likely permanent thyroid dysfunction will require lifelong levothyroxine replacement.

3. STEROID TAPER — NEAR COMPLETION

Patient remains on prednisone 2.5 mg/day — essentially near-physiologic, the residual tail of the long steroid taper initiated for grade 3 colitis. He has been on this dose for approximately 2 months without symptoms of colitis recurrence. AM cortisol 14.8 mcg/dL is adequate, suggesting the adrenal axis is recovering appropriately and that further taper should be safe.

Plan:
- Taper prednisone to 2.5 mg every other day for 2-4 weeks, then discontinue if well tolerated and AM cortisol repeats normal.

- No evidence of colitis recurrence; no GI symptoms. Continue omeprazole while on residual prednisone and taper off once prednisone is fully discontinued.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Ellison is a 57-year-old White male with a diagnosis of stage IV, M1c, BRAF V600E-mutant nodular melanoma of the upper back, initially presenting at age 55 with extensive bilateral axillary and left cervical lymphadenopathy, multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm), widespread cutaneous and subcutaneous satellite nodules on the trunk and extremities, and a right axillary conglomerate nodal mass measuring 4.8 cm. LDH at presentation was significantly elevated at 642 U/L. He was ECOG 1 at the time of metastatic diagnosis.

Given the high hepatic disease burden and markedly elevated LDH, a decision was made at multidisciplinary tumor board to initiate first-line targeted therapy with dabra (Tafinlar) 150 mg twice daily and Mekinist (Mekinist) 2 mg once daily for rapid disease control. He achieved an excellent early partial response -- palpable nodules regressed nearly completely by 6 weeks, and formal CT imaging at 3 months confirmed substantial shrinkage of hepatic and nodal target lesions, with LDH normalizing to the 160-180 range. Response subsequently stabilized at approximately 6 months. Progression was confirmed at approximately 8 months with significant re-growth of axillary and hepatic lesions, new hepatic foci, new subcutaneous deposits, and new right external iliac lymphadenopathy. LDH rebounded to 589 U/L. Acquired resistance biopsy and repeat molecular profiling demonstrated BRAF gene amplification, an emergent MEK1 p.P124S resistance mutation, and a new PIK3CA p.E545K mutation, consistent with the expected acquired resistance landscape for BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy.

Tafinlar/tram was discontinued. After confirmatory brain MRI (negative for CNS involvement), he was transitioned to second-line Yervoy (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg IV plus nivo (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (CheckMate 067 dosing) with plans for 4 induction cycles followed by Opdivo maintenance. He completed cycles 1 and 2 with manageable grade 1-2 fatigue, pruritus, and mild rash, and early LDH improvement to 412 U/L. After cycle 3, he was hospitalized with grade 3 immune-mediated colitis: 8-10 watery bowel movements daily, severe abdominal cramping, and dehydration. Infectious workup was negative. Flexible sigmoidoscopy confirmed immune-mediated colitis with diffuse erythema, erosions, and ulcerations. He required IV methylprednisolone followed by an infliximab infusion for incomplete steroid response, with eventual complete mucosal healing confirmed on follow-up colonoscopy. ipi was permanently discontinued. He was eventually rechallenged with Opdivo as single-agent maintenance (480 mg IV every 4 weeks) after steroid taper to prednisone 10 mg/day and full GI symptom resolution.

Since resuming nivo maintenance approximately 6 months ago, Mr. Ellison has demonstrated an excellent and progressively deepening response. CT restaging at approximately 5 months after immunotherapy initiation showed a partial response with 53-57% reductions in target lesions. A subsequent CT at approximately 9 months after immunotherapy start showed continued deepening response, with the right axillary mass down to 0.9 cm, hepatic segment VII lesion at 0.6 cm, segment II lesion at 0.4 cm, and complete resolution of subcutaneous nodules. The overall reduction in target lesion burden from pre-immunotherapy baseline was approximately 77%. Brain MRI at the same timepoint confirmed no CNS involvement.

He developed grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism approximately 8 months into immunotherapy (TSH 14.2 mIU/L, low free T4), with positive anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies. Levothyroxine was uptitrated to 75 mcg/day, with normalization of TSH to 3.2 mIU/L and resolution of symptoms. nivo was continued without interruption.

He now presents for cycle 10, approximately 10 months from immunotherapy initiation. He reports substantial improvement in his overall sense of well-being, no GI symptoms, and has returned to modified work as a landscaper on a light-duty basis. He remains on levothyroxine 75 mcg/day and low-dose prednisone 2.5 mg/day (tapering).

The visualized calvarium and skull base demonstrate no abnormal FDG-avid foci. There is physiologic symmetric activity within the salivary glands and oropharyngeal soft tissues. Bilateral cervical lymph node regions are surveyed. The previously described hypermetabolic left cervical level II and III lymph nodes demonstrated on the baseline PET-CT (SUVmax 6.8-9.2 at initial staging) are no longer identifiable as distinct metabolically active foci. The left neck demonstrates no lymph nodes meeting size criteria for concern and no foci of abnormal FDG uptake above background soft tissue activity. Incidentally, there is mild symmetric FDG uptake in the masseter musculature, favoring physiologic jaw muscle activity. No FDG-avid parotid, submandibular, or cervical nodal disease.

THORAX:
The lung parenchyma demonstrates no focal FDG-avid lesions. No pulmonary nodules of concern are identified on the CT component. The mediastinum shows no pathologically enlarged or metabolically active lymph nodes. Bilateral hilar regions demonstrate no abnormal FDG uptake. There is physiologic cardiac and great vessel blood pool activity. The pleural spaces are clear bilaterally. Bony thoracic cage and visualized soft tissues of the chest wall demonstrate no suspicious FDG avidity.

RIGHT AXILLARY REGION (prior site of dominant nodal disease):
At baseline staging, the right axillary conglomerate nodal mass measured 4.8 x 3.9 cm with an SUVmax of 18.7, representing the metabolically most active site of disease at that time. On the most recent prior CT (9-month restaging), this had decreased to 0.9 cm in short axis. On the current examination, a small rounded focus of soft tissue density is identified within the right axilla measuring approximately 0.8 cm in greatest dimension. The FDG activity associated with this residual focus is markedly attenuated, with a peak SUVmax of 2.1, which is only marginally above mediastinal blood pool background activity (SUVmax approximately 1.7) and is well below the threshold conventionally considered indicative of viable metabolically active malignancy. The morphology and density of this focus on the CT component, in conjunction with the near-background metabolic activity, are most consistent with a small fibrotic or necrotic residual nodal remnant rather than viable tumor. No other axillary nodal activity is identified on either side.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver:
At baseline, the liver harbored at least six discrete FDG-avid metastatic deposits, the dominant lesion in segment VII with an SUVmax of 14.3. On the current examination, the liver appears homogeneous without discrete hypodense lesions on the CT component and without any focally increased FDG uptake. The previously described segment VII lesion site demonstrates only faint, minimally heterogeneous hepatic parenchymal activity in the range of SUVmax 1.8, which does not reach a threshold for metabolically active disease and falls within expected hepatic background. This is substantially reduced from the last CT measurement of 0.6 cm at the 9-month timepoint. No FDG-avid lesions are identified in segment II, segment IV, or segment VI, all of which were previously involved. The overall hepatic metabolic activity is indistinguishable from normal background parenchymal uptake. There is no periportal or porta hepatis nodal FDG activity.

Biliary system, gallbladder, and spleen:
The gallbladder and biliary system demonstrate no FDG-avid abnormality. The spleen is normal in size with physiologic homogeneous uptake. No splenic lesions identified.

Pancreas, adrenal glands, kidneys:
The pancreas demonstrates physiologic activity pattern without focal abnormality. The adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology bilaterally with no FDG-avid lesions. The kidneys show expected physiologic FDG excretion into the collecting systems bilaterally. No renal parenchymal FDG-avid foci of concern.

Retroperitoneum and pelvic lymph nodes:
The retroperitoneal lymph node chains are surveyed. No pathologically enlarged or metabolically active retroperitoneal nodes are identified. The right external iliac lymph node previously measuring 1.2 cm at pre-immunotherapy baseline with subsequent decrease to 0.4 cm on the 9-month CT is no longer individually identifiable as a distinct lesion; the right external iliac chain shows no FDG uptake above background. Pelvic sidewall, inguinal, and common iliac regions demonstrate no abnormal FDG activity.

Gastrointestinal TRACT:
Physiologic FDG activity is noted throughout the gastrointestinal tract. There is no focal wall thickening or mucosal FDG uptake pattern to suggest active colitis along the visualized large bowel. This is consistent with the patient's clinical and endoscopic history of resolved immune-mediated colitis. No abnormal bowel FDG activity.

SKIN AND SUBCUTANEOUS SOFT TISSUES:
Extensive prior involvement with multiple cutaneous and subcutaneous satellite nodules on the trunk and bilateral upper extremities (at least 9 discrete hypermetabolic foci at baseline, SUVmax range 4.1-8.9) is surveyed. The current examination demonstrates no FDG-avid cutaneous or subcutaneous lesions anywhere on the trunk, back, or extremities. The dorsal skin of the upper back overlying the site of the primary lesion excision demonstrates no abnormal FDG activity. No satellite nodule recurrence is identified. Right forearm demonstrates post-surgical changes consistent with the prior wide local excision for cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma; no FDG-avid recurrence at this site.

SKELETAL SURVEY:
The axial and appendicular skeleton is surveyed on both the PET and CT components. No FDG-avid osseous lesions are identified. No lytic or sclerotic lesions are seen on the CT component to suggest bone metastases. No pathologic fractures visualized.

BRAIN:
The brain is not fully evaluated on this examination given that no contrast-enhanced MRI was performed concurrently. The visualized cranium on the CT component demonstrates no obvious lytic or destructive bony lesions. Intracranial FDG distribution appears symmetric and physiologic without focal cortical or subcortical hypermetabolic foci. For definitive assessment of intracranial disease status, dedicated contrast-enhanced brain MRI remains the appropriate modality per protocol.

BACKGROUND AND MISCELLANEOUS:
Physiologic FDG activity is present in the brain, myocardium, liver parenchyma, spleen, kidneys, collecting systems, and large bowel. Symmetric physiologic muscular activity in bilateral lower extremities. No unexpected FDG-avid foci in the thorax, abdomen, pelvis, or extremities outside of those already described. No pleural or pericardial effusions. No ascites.

IMPRESSION:

1. Near-complete metabolic response to immunotherapy in the setting of BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV metastatic melanoma. Comparing to the baseline staging PET-CT, which demonstrated widespread high-level metabolic activity at multiple nodal, hepatic, and cutaneous sites (right axillary mass SUVmax 18.7, hepatic lesions up to SUVmax 14.3, numerous subcutaneous nodules), the current examination demonstrates striking and thorough suppression of metabolic disease activity across all previously involved anatomic regions.

2. Small residual right axillary soft tissue focus (approximately 0.8 cm) with near-background FDG activity (SUVmax 2.1), markedly reduced from prior. The extremely low metabolic activity of this focus is most in keeping with fibrotic or necrotic residual tissue rather than viable tumor deposit; active disease at this site cannot be definitively excluded on imaging alone but appears unlikely.

3. No FDG-avid hepatic lesions identified. Previously extensive hepatic involvement across multiple segments now demonstrates only trace faint background-level activity in the region of the prior segment VII lesion, below any threshold for active disease.

4. Complete resolution of FDG-avid left cervical nodal disease, right external iliac nodal disease, and all previously demonstrated cutaneous and subcutaneous metabolic foci.

5. No new sites of FDG-avid disease identified anywhere in the body.

6. No evidence of metabolically active disease in the visualized intracranial structures; dedicated brain MRI recommended for comprehensive CNS surveillance per protocol.

7. Bowel activity pattern is reassuring without features to suggest active immune-mediated colitis.

Overall, the findings are consistent with a deep and durable treatment response to nivolumab-based immunotherapy, representing one of the most favorable metabolic response patterns that can be documented in this disease setting.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Ellison carries a diagnosis of nodular melanoma of the upper back, diagnosed at age 55, with initial staging revealing extensive locoregional and distant metastatic disease: bilateral axillary and left cervical lymphadenopathy, six discrete hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm in segment VII), and multiple cutaneous and subcutaneous satellite nodules across the trunk and extremities. LDH at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 642 U/L. His tumor harbored a BRAF p.V600E mutation confirmed both by immunohistochemistry (VE1) and comprehensive 523-gene NGS panel, with co-occurring alterations including CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN p.R130* loss-of-function mutation, TP53 p.R175H gain-of-function mutation, TERT promoter mutation, RAC1 p.P29S, CCND1 amplification, and MYC amplification. TMB was intermediate-high at 18.4 mut/Mb; MSS. PD-L1 TPS 25%, CPS 38 by 28-8 assay.

Given the high-volume hepatic metastatic burden and dramatically elevated LDH at presentation — both factors favoring rapid disease control — he was initiated on first-line dabrafenib 150 mg BID / trametinib 2 mg QD (dabrafen/tram combination) with an excellent early response: LDH plummeted from 642 to 167 U/L by three months, and cross-sectional imaging confirmed robust partial response with target lesion reductions of 60-67%. His clinical course on dabrafenib/trametinib was complicated by a class-effect cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cuSCC) of the right forearm at approximately 4 months, managed with wide local excision with clear margins; he also experienced grade 1-2 fatigue and intermittent low-grade pyrexia throughout. Disease remained in stable partial response through months 6-7, but follow-up imaging at approximately 8 months demonstrated unequivocal radiographic progression, with the right axillary mass growing from 1.7 to 3.4 cm, hepatic segment VII lesion from 1.0 to 2.8 cm, appearance of two new hepatic lesions in segments IV and VI, multiple new subcutaneous nodules, and a new right external iliac lymph node — accompanied by resurgent LDH to 589 U/L. A resistance biopsy of a new abdominal subcutaneous nodule was performed, and repeat NGS confirmed acquired BRAF amplification (~8x copy number gain), a MAP2K1 p.P124S MEK1 resistance mutation (reducing drug binding), and a newly emergent PIK3CA p.E545K mutation, mechanistically explaining the resistance phenotype.

He was transitioned to second-line ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV q3 weeks plus nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV q3 weeks (CheckMate 067 dosing) after pre-immunotherapy brain MRI confirmed no CNS involvement. His immunotherapy course was complicated significantly at approximately 9 weeks (following cycle 3 of ipi/nivo) by grade 3 immune-mediated colitis, presenting with 8-10 watery bowel movements per day, severe cramping, dehydration, and fever. He was hospitalized and treated with high-dose IV methylprednisolone, and subsequently required infliximab 5 mg/kg x1 dose given incomplete steroid response. Both ipilimumab and nivolumab were held during this period; ipilimumab was permanently discontinued per protocol given the grade 3 severity. He responded well to infliximab — stool frequency normalized — and prednisone was successfully tapered over 8 weeks. Confirmatory colonoscopy at 10 weeks post-colitis onset demonstrated mucosal healing.

After approximately 4 months from immunotherapy initiation (following complete steroid taper), nivolumab was resumed as single-agent maintenance at the flat dose of 480 mg IV q4 weeks. He has now been on nivolumab maintenance for approximately 10 additional months. His first restaging CT at 5 months from immunotherapy start showed partial response with 53-57% reductions in target lesions. Deepening response was documented on subsequent imaging at 9 months (overall 77% reduction in target lesion sum from pre-immunotherapy baseline). A PET-CT at approximately 12 months confirmed near-complete metabolic response: no FDG-avid hepatic lesions, only a 0.8 cm residual right axillary soft tissue density with background-level SUVmax 2.1, felt to represent fibrotic/necrotic residual without viable disease.

His immunotherapy-related toxicities beyond the grade 3 colitis have included immune-mediated hypothyroidism, diagnosed at approximately 8 months into immunotherapy when TSH rose to 14.2 mIU/L with suppressed free T4. Anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies were both positive. He was uptitrated to levothyroxine 75 mcg/day with normalization of thyroid function (TSH 2.8-3.2 mIU/L on replacement) and resolution of symptoms (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, mild cognitive slowing). No corticosteroids were required.

He returns today having received his most recent nivolumab infusion 3 weeks ago. He reports feeling well, with markedly improved energy and quality of life compared to diagnosis. He has resumed modified light-duty landscaping work. He denies any gastrointestinal symptoms.

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD REVIEW SUMMARY

Mr. Ellison was presented at today's Melanoma Multidisciplinary Tumor Board attended by Medical Oncology, Surgical Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Diagnostic Radiology, Dermatology, and Pathology. The case was reviewed in full, including the near-complete metabolic PET-CT response, the patient's functional status, toxicity history, and current nivolumab maintenance.

Key discussion points:

1. Treatment holiday vs. continuation: There was robust discussion regarding whether to consider a planned immunotherapy holiday given the near-complete metabolic response. Arguments in favor of continuing: (a) initial M1c disease with very high-volume hepatic and nodal burden and elevated LDH — these features historically associate with higher relapse risk even in responders; (b) the patient initially received only partial combination (ipilimumab had to be permanently discontinued after 3 cycles due to grade 3 colitis, leaving nivolumab as the sole active agent for the majority of treatment); (c) published long-term outcomes from CheckMate 067 suggest durable remission rates of approximately 20% at 5 years, but durability of responses appears to favor continued treatment in M1c disease particularly when complete response has not been formally declared; (d) nivolumab is reasonably well-tolerated at present with no active grade ≥2 toxicities. Arguments for holiday: (a) near-complete metabolic response; (b) ongoing hypothyroidism as irreversible immune-related endocrinopathy; (c) patient quality of life and preference. Consensus recommendation: Continue nivolumab 480 mg IV q4 weeks indefinitely, with shared decision-making. The residual 0.8 cm axillary soft tissue with low-level metabolic activity, while likely inactive, has not met criteria for complete response, and the board was not comfortable recommending holiday in the setting of M1c disease without formal CR.

2. Role of re-biopsy: Re-biopsy of residual axillary soft tissue was discussed but deferred. The board agreed the PET-CT profile is consistent with treatment-related fibrosis, and biopsy risk (including potential bleeding and wound complications in a previously irradiated nodal field) was felt to outweigh benefit at this time.

3. Surveillance imaging interval: Imaging every 4 months on nivolumab maintenance endorsed. Brain MRI every 6 months endorsed. PET-CT to be repeated at 18-month immunotherapy mark (approximately 4 months from today) to further characterize residual axillary soft tissue.

4. Dermatology: Annual full-body dermatologic examination recommended, with particular attention to prior cuSCC site and any new BRAF inhibitor-independent squamous lesions.

SOMATIC MUTATIONS — TIER I/II (Actionable and Clinically Significant)

1. BRAF p.V600E (c.1799T>A)
   Gene Function: Serine/threonine kinase; MAPK pathway
   Variant Class: Missense mutation
   Allele Frequency: 48.2%
   Exon: 15
   Classification: ONCOGENIC — Tier I (FDA-approved therapy available)
   Clinical Significance: The BRAF p.V600E substitution (valine to glutamic acid at codon 600) is the most common driver mutation in cutaneous melanoma, present in approximately 50% of cases. This variant constitutively activates BRAF kinase signaling, driving RAS-independent activation of the MEK-ERK cascade and promoting tumor cell proliferation and survival. This specific alteration is the predictive biomarker for FDA-approved BRAF-targeted therapies including vemurafenib (Zelboraf), dabrafenib (Tafinlar), and encorafenib (Braftovi), as well as combination BRAF+MEK inhibitor regimens (dabrafenib + trametinib [Tafinlar + Mekinist]; vemurafenib + cobimetinib [Zelboraf + Cotellic]; encorafenib + binimetinib [Braftovi + Mektovi]). Allele frequency of 48.2% is consistent with a heterozygous mutation in a high-purity tumor specimen, with no evidence of allelic imbalance at this locus (see copy number data). BRAF amplification not detected at this timepoint.

2. PTEN p.R130* (c.388C>T)
   Gene Function: Tumor suppressor; PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway antagonist (lipid phosphatase)
   Variant Class: Nonsense mutation (premature stop codon)
   Allele Frequency: 41.1%
   Exon: 5
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Loss of function, Tier II
   Clinical Significance: PTEN p.R130* introduces a premature termination codon, resulting in a truncated, non-functional protein and loss of phosphatase activity. PTEN loss is among the most frequently co-occurring alterations in BRAF-mutant melanoma (present in 10–30% of cases) and results in constitutive activation of the PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling axis, which operates in parallel to and independently of the MAPK cascade. PTEN loss has been associated with reduced responsiveness to BRAF inhibitor monotherapy and to anti-PD-1 immunotherapy in some preclinical and clinical data. The co-occurrence of PTEN loss with BRAF V600E creates a two-hit oncogenic landscape that may contribute to aggressive clinical behavior. No evidence of PTEN homozygous deletion by copy number analysis at this locus (hemizygous loss with somatic mutation of retained allele suspected, consistent with VAF of 41.1% in 72% tumor).

3. TP53 p.R175H (c.524G>A)
   Gene Function: Tumor suppressor; cell cycle checkpoint, DNA damage response, apoptosis
   Variant Class: Missense mutation (gain-of-function)
   Allele Frequency: 39.8%
   Exon: 5
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Gain-of-function, Tier II
   Clinical Significance: TP53 p.R175H is one of the canonical gain-of-function p53 "hotspot" mutations and among the most commonly identified TP53 alterations in human cancers. Unlike simple loss-of-function mutations, p.R175H confers oncogenic gain-of-function properties, including aberrant transcriptional activation of pro-oncogenic genes, disruption of wild-type p53 remaining in the cell (dominant-negative effect), impaired apoptotic response, promotion of cell migration and invasion, and interaction with chromatin remodeling complexes. The allele frequency of 39.8% is consistent with a clonal, heterozygous mutation in the dominant tumor clone. Concurrent CDKN2A loss (see below) eliminates an additional cell cycle safeguard, compounding the effect of p53 dysfunction.

4. RAC1 p.P29S (c.85C>T)
   Gene Function: Rho GTPase; cytoskeletal organization, cell migration, MAPK signaling
   Variant Class: Missense mutation
   Allele Frequency: 11.4%
   Exon: 2
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC / Tier IIB — subclonal
   Clinical Significance: RAC1 p.P29S is the third most common mutation in cutaneous melanoma after BRAF and NRAS, present in approximately 4–7% of primary melanomas. This mutation is the canonical UV-signature change (C>T at a dipyrimidine site, consistent with SBS7 signature), creating a fast-cycling, constitutively active RAC1 GTPase that promotes melanocyte proliferation and transformation. The relatively lower allele frequency (11.4%) suggests a subclonal event, potentially arising later during clonal evolution. RAC1 p.P29S does not have approved targeted therapies but is recognized as a driver in melanoma. Its presence alongside a UV-dominant mutational signature supports UV exposure as the primary mutagenic etiology. No approved targeted therapies are available for RAC1 p.P29S at this time; investigational RAC1 inhibitors are in early-phase clinical trials.

5. TERT promoter c.-124C>T
   Gene Function: Telomerase reverse transcriptase; telomere maintenance
   Variant Class: Promoter mutation
   Allele Frequency: 52.3%
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Tier IIB (prognostic significance)
   Clinical Significance: TERT promoter mutations (c.-124C>T and c.-146C>T) are the most common somatic mutations identified in cutaneous melanoma (found in 70–80% of metastatic cases) and are considered an early, clonal event in melanocyte transformation. The c.-124C>T variant creates a novel ETS transcription factor binding site, resulting in dramatically upregulated TERT expression and telomerase reactivation, conferring effective cellular immortality. In melanoma, TERT promoter mutation is strongly associated with aggressive disease biology, metastatic potential, and adverse prognosis, particularly in combination with other driver mutations such as BRAF V600E and CDKN2A loss. The high allele frequency (52.3%) is consistent with a clonal mutation present in the predominant tumor population. No directly targetable therapeutic implication.

COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS

6. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion (chr9p21.3)
   Gene Function: Tumor suppressor; encodes p16/INK4A (CDK4/6 inhibitor) and p14/ARF (p53 pathway activator)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Tier II
   Clinical Significance: Homozygous deletion of CDKN2A (9p21.3) simultaneously abrogates both the p16-CDK4/6-RB cell cycle axis and the p14ARF-MDM2-p53 tumor suppressor pathway. CDKN2A homozygous deletion is present in approximately 30–40% of cutaneous melanomas and represents one of the most impactful tumor suppressor losses in melanoma pathogenesis. The combined effect of homozygous CDKN2A deletion and TP53 p.R175H gain-of-function mutation renders this tumor essentially devoid of functional p53 pathway control and CDK4/6-RB checkpoint activity. Note: loss of p14ARF further compromises MDM2 regulation, potentially amplifying the oncogenic effects of the co-occurring TP53 gain-of-function mutation.
   Estimated Copy Number: 0 (biallelic deletion confirmed; log2 ratio -3.2)

7. CCND1 Amplification (chr11q13.3)
   Gene Function: Cyclin D1; cell cycle progression, CDK4/6 activation, RB phosphorylation
   Estimated Copy Number: ~9 copies (log2 ratio +2.1)
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC — Tier IIB
   Clinical Significance: CCND1 amplification results in overexpression of Cyclin D1, which partners with CDK4 and CDK6 to phosphorylate and inactivate the RB tumor suppressor, driving cell cycle progression from G1 into S phase. In the context of concurrent CDKN2A homozygous deletion (which removes the endogenous CDK4/6 inhibitor p16), CCND1 amplification and CDKN2A loss functionally converge to completely deregulate the CDK4/6-RB axis. CCND1 amplification in melanoma has been associated with worse prognosis and more proliferative disease biology.

8. MYC Amplification (chr8q24.21)
   Gene Function: Transcription factor; cell growth, proliferation, metabolism, ribosome biogenesis, apoptosis suppression
   Estimated Copy Number: ~7 copies (log2 ratio +1.8)
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC — Tier IIB
   Clinical Significance: MYC amplification is a recurrent copy number gain in metastatic melanoma, observed in approximately 15–20% of advanced cases. MYC overexpression drives an extensive transcriptional program promoting tumor growth, suppresses apoptotic signaling, and cooperates with BRAF V600E to accelerate MAPK-driven proliferation. MYC amplification has been associated with more aggressive disease course and may contribute to resistance mechanisms. There are no approved MYC-targeted therapies; BET bromodomain inhibitors targeting BRD4-dependent MYC transcription are in clinical investigation.

TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TMB: 18.4 mutations per megabase (mut/Mb)
Interpretation: INTERMEDIATE-HIGH
Context: TMB-high threshold (≥10 mut/Mb per FDA pembrolizumab indication) is met. Cutaneous melanoma as a class has among the highest TMBs of any solid tumor, driven by UV-induced mutagenesis (C>T transitions at dipyrimidine sites). This TMB of 18.4 mut/Mb places the tumor in the intermediate-high range for melanoma (median TMB for metastatic melanoma is approximately 15–20 mut/Mb). High TMB is associated with increased neoantigen burden and improved responses to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy in multiple tumor types. Combined with a CPS of 38 and TPS of 25% for PD-L1, this tumor has features that may be associated with immunotherapy responsiveness.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Dominant Signature: SBS7a (UV signature, C>T substitutions at TpC sites, 58.4% of mutations)

Interpretation: The mutational landscape is overwhelmingly dominated by ultraviolet radiation-associated signatures (SBS7a and SBS7b together account for 82.5% of mutations), consistent with cumulative UV-induced DNA damage as the primary etiologic mechanism. This signature pattern is characteristic of sun-exposed cutaneous melanoma arising in the context of outdoor occupational and recreational UV exposure, as reported for this patient. The high proportion of UV-signature mutations is consistent with the elevated TMB and supports the primary diagnosis of cutaneous melanoma. No APOBEC (SBS2/SBS13), homologous recombination deficiency (SBS3), tobacco (SBS4), or other exogenous carcinogen signatures are detected.

GERMLINE DISCLAIMER
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This is a somatic tumor-only assay. The allele frequencies of detected variants (BRAF p.V600E AF 48.2%, PTEN p.R130* AF 41.1%, TP53 p.R175H AF 39.8%, TERT promoter c.-124C>T AF 52.3%) are consistent with clonal somatic mutations in a high-cellularity tumor specimen and are not consistent with germline alterations. Constitutional BRAF mutation is not suspected. However, formal germline testing is recommended for BRCA1/2, CDKN2A (given family history of melanoma in maternal aunt), and BAP1 to assess hereditary cancer predisposition, and genetic counseling referral is strongly advised.

Note: CDKN2A germline pathogenic variants are found in approximately 20–40% of familial melanoma kindreds; the family history of melanoma in this patient's maternal aunt warrants formal germline assessment.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTEGRATED MOLECULAR INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This metastatic cutaneous melanoma harbors a complex genomic landscape with multiple co-occurring oncogenic and tumor suppressor alterations. The dominant driver is BRAF p.V600E, an actionable Tier I alteration with direct therapeutic implications. Concurrent alterations include:

- Loss of CDKN2A (homozygous deletion): abrogates G1/S checkpoint via p16-CDK4/6-RB and p14ARF-MDM2-p53 pathways
- PTEN p.R130* (loss of function): constitutive PI3K-AKT pathway activation, operating as a parallel survival signal to MAPK
- TP53 p.R175H (gain-of-function): dominant oncogenic p53 activity compounding loss of p14ARF-p53 regulation
- TERT promoter c.-124C>T: clonal, associated with aggressive biology and poor prognosis in melanoma
- RAC1 p.P29S: subclonal UV-signature GTPase activator; no currently actionable target
- CCND1 amplification and MYC amplification: convergent copy number alterations driving cell cycle dysregulation and proliferation

The combination of BRAF V600E, CDKN2A deletion, PTEN loss, CCND1 amplification, and MYC amplification defines a genomically aggressive tumor with convergent MAPK hyperactivation and cell cycle checkpoint abrogation. PTEN loss and PI3K pathway activation may represent a baseline co-existing resistance mechanism that could limit depth or duration of BRAF/MEK inhibitor response over time; however, BRAF-targeted therapy is the standard-of-care first-line option for BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma with high disease burden. The TMB of 18.4 mut/Mb and PD-L1 TPS 25% / CPS 38 suggest that immunotherapy (anti-PD-1 or combination checkpoint blockade) may be effective if/when BRAF-targeted therapy is exhausted or as an alternative treatment sequence.

The UV-dominant mutational signature is consistent with occupational UV exposure history and supports the clinical and pathologic diagnosis of primary cutaneous melanoma.

LIMITATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
• This assay detects alterations in the 523 genes on the panel; alterations in genes not included on the panel will not be detected.
• FFPE-derived DNA may introduce artifactual C>T transitions; stringent bioinformatic filtering for FFPE artifact has been applied. All reported variants exceed artifact thresholds.

Specifically, no punctate, nodular, ring-enhancing, or miliary enhancing foci are detected throughout the cerebral cortex, subcortical white matter, deep gray nuclei, brainstem, or cerebellum. The post-contrast sequences were reviewed at multiple window/level settings to ensure no subtle lesions were overlooked. There is no abnormal sulcal or pachymeningeal enhancement.

WHITE MATTER:
The supratentorial and infratentorial white matter is within normal limits. No confluent, focal, or periventricular signal abnormality is present on FLAIR or T2-weighted sequences beyond what might be expected for age. No demyelinating or infiltrative process identified.

DEEP GRAY STRUCTURES AND BRAINSTEM:
The basal ganglia (caudate, putamen, globus pallidus), thalami, internal capsules, and hypothalamus are symmetric and unremarkable in signal and morphology. The brainstem -- midbrain, pons, and medulla -- is normal in contour, signal intensity, and enhancement pattern. The cerebral peduncles, corticospinal tracts, and tegmentum are intact. No mass lesion or infiltrative process.

CEREBELLUM:
The cerebellar hemispheres and vermis are symmetrical and demonstrate normal foliation without signal abnormality. No enhancing lesion. The dentate nuclei are normal in signal. No posterior fossa mass effect.

VENTRICLES AND CSF SPACES:
The lateral ventricles are symmetric and of normal caliber. Third and fourth ventricles are midline and unremarkable. No hydrocephalus. The cerebral aqueduct is patent. The basal cisterns are open. Cerebrospinal fluid signal is normal throughout the ventricular system and subarachnoid spaces.

MIDLINE STRUCTURES:
No shift of midline structures. The falx cerebri and tentorium cerebelli are unremarkable. No herniation.

HEMORRHAGE / BLOOD PRODUCTS:
No evidence of acute, subacute, or chronic hemorrhage on gradient echo or SWI sequences. No hemosiderin deposition or microhemorrhages. This is particularly relevant given melanoma's known propensity for hemorrhagic metastases; none are identified on susceptibility-weighted imaging.

SELLA AND PITUITARY GLAND:
The pituitary gland is normal in size and signal intensity, with appropriate enhancement of the posterior pituitary bright spot. The pituitary stalk is midline and not thickened. No sellar or suprasellar mass. The optic chiasm is unremarkable.

ORBITS (PARTIALLY VISUALIZED):
The retrobulbar fat and intraorbital structures are normal bilaterally on the sequences provided. No intraorbital or retroorbital mass. The optic nerves demonstrate normal caliber and signal bilaterally without evidence of perineural spread or infiltration.

SKULL BASE AND CALVARIUM:
The calvarium is intact without erosive lesion, permeative change, or abnormal marrow signal to suggest osseous metastatic involvement. The skull base foramina are patent bilaterally. No destructive lesion. The mastoid air cells are normally aerated.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
The major intracranial arteries and dural venous sinuses are visualized and appear patent on the sequences available. The superior sagittal sinus, transverse sinuses, and sigmoid sinuses are patent without thrombosis or abnormal signal. No flow void abnormality.

SOFT TISSUES OF THE SCALP:
The scalp and subgaleal tissues are unremarkable. No scalp nodule, cutaneous metastasis, or abnormal enhancement in the regions imaged.

LEPTOMENINGEAL ASSESSMENT:
Specific attention was paid to evaluation of the leptomeningeal surfaces, including the sulci, sylvian fissures, cerebellar folds, and basal cisterns. No abnormal leptomeningeal enhancement is identified. There is no evidence of carcinomatous meningitis.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No intracranial metastatic disease identified. No parenchymal, meningeal, or leptomeningeal lesions are present on this gadolinium-enhanced study.

2. No hemorrhagic foci on susceptibility-weighted imaging, which is reassuring given the known tendency for melanoma metastases to be hemorrhagic.

3. Normal brain MRI without mass effect, midline shift, hydrocephalus, or evidence of leptomeningeal involvement.

4. Intact calvarium without osseous lesion in the fields imaged.

5. This baseline examination establishes no central nervous system metastatic burden at initial staging and provides an important reference point for future surveillance.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Ellison is a 55-year-old White male with newly diagnosed Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, presenting today for his first on-treatment follow-up visit approximately 6 weeks after initiation of dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily and trametinib 2 mg once daily. Visit purpose: assessment of treatment tolerance, early response evaluation, and adverse event monitoring.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Ellison was diagnosed approximately 6-7 weeks ago with Stage IV metastatic melanoma arising from a primary lesion on the upper back. The primary lesion was a 4.2 cm ulcerated nodular melanoma (Breslow 6.1 mm, Clark level V, mitotic rate 6/mm², lymphovascular invasion present, deep margin positive). Staging workup at the time of diagnosis demonstrated extensive regional and distant metastatic disease: bilateral axillary and left cervical lymphadenopathy with the right axillary conglomerate mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 cm on CT, six discrete hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm in segment VII), and multiple subcutaneous and cutaneous satellite nodules on the trunk and extremities. PET-CT at baseline confirmed widespread hypermetabolic disease burden (SUVmax 18.7 in the right axillary mass, SUVmax 14.3 in the dominant hepatic lesion, at least 9 hypermetabolic subcutaneous nodules). Brain MRI at baseline was negative for intracranial metastases. LDH at baseline was 642 U/L (approximately 2x the upper limit of normal), which was an important prognostic factor.

Molecular profiling of the right axillary lymph node metastasis by a 523-gene NGS panel confirmed BRAF p.V600E mutation (allele frequency 48.2%), as well as CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN p.R130* loss-of-function mutation, TP53 p.R175H gain-of-function mutation, TERT promoter mutation, and RAC1 p.P29S mutation. Tumor mutational burden was intermediate-high at 18.4 mut/Mb. PD-L1 by 28-8 assay showed tumor proportion score 25% and combined positive score 38. Microsatellite stable.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, the decision was made to initiate first-line targeted therapy with dabrafenib/trametinib given the high hepatic disease burden, markedly elevated LDH, and the need for rapid disease control. The combination was started at standard doses (dabrafenib 150 mg PO BID, trametinib 2 mg PO daily). Baseline echocardiogram showed LVEF 62%, baseline liver function tests showed AST 78 U/L and ALT 54 U/L.

Today, approximately 6 weeks into treatment, the patient presents reporting a notably favorable response. He describes significant shrinkage of the subcutaneous nodules he had been palpating on his own — he states that the lumps he could feel on his trunk and arms have nearly all disappeared. He reports improved appetite and substantially improved energy level. He has been able to return to some light household activities, which he had been unable to do at the time of diagnosis. He denies significant pain. He does describe one episode approximately 2-3 weeks ago during which he developed a fever to approximately 38.5°C (101.3°F) associated with chills and malaise that resolved within 24 hours following a dose hold of dabrafenib and acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours as needed. He held the dabrafenib for one day and then resumed without difficulty. He endorses mild ongoing fatigue (rates 3/10 in severity, not impacting daily function) and notices some thickened, rough skin on the palms of both hands.

LDH: 214 U/L — markedly improved from baseline of 642 U/L; this represents a 67% reduction and is now just above the upper limit of normal (approximately 190-200 U/L at our institution). Trending strongly toward normalization.

Cardiac: ECG obtained today (per protocol for QTc monitoring on dabrafenib/trametinib). QTc: 428 ms — within normal limits, no QTc prolongation. No morphological changes from prior tracing.

Echocardiogram (obtained within the past 2 weeks per protocol): LVEF 60% — stable from baseline LVEF of 62%; no new wall motion abnormalities; no pericardial effusion. No significant change.

Thyroid function: TSH within normal limits (not formally rechecked today; deferred to next visit per protocol).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new restaging imaging obtained at this visit per protocol (first formal restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis scheduled at the 3-month mark, approximately 4-6 weeks from now). Clinical response assessment based on physical examination and laboratory trends as above.

Most recent imaging on file: Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (at diagnosis) — right axillary conglomerate mass 4.8 x 3.9 cm, six hepatic metastases, multiple subcutaneous nodules, left cervical adenopathy. PET-CT (at diagnosis) — widespread hypermetabolic disease, SUVmax up to 18.7. Brain MRI (at diagnosis) — no intracranial metastases.

Clinical response surrogate today: LDH decline from 642 U/L to 214 U/L, near-complete resolution of palpable subcutaneous nodules, and significant reduction in right axillary mass on examination — all strongly supportive of rapid early BRAF/MEK inhibitor response.

---

ASSESSMENT

Mr. Ellison is a 55-year-old male with Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic nodular melanoma (high-burden hepatic, nodal, and soft tissue disease) who is approximately 6 weeks into first-line targeted therapy with dabrafenib (Tafinlar) and trametinib (Mekinist). He is demonstrating a rapid and clinically significant early treatment response. This is both expected and gratifying given the natural history of BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma treated with combined BRAF/MEK inhibition.

Key findings today:

1. RAPID EARLY RESPONSE — BRAF/MEK inhibitor: The clinical picture is entirely consistent with the well-characterized rapid early response seen with dabrafenib/trametinib in BRAF V600E-positive melanoma. Palpable subcutaneous nodules have nearly completely resolved; right axillary mass has approximately 50% clinical reduction in size; LDH has fallen dramatically from 642 to 214 U/L (over 65% decrease). This is a reassuring and meaningful early response by all available surrogate markers.

2. ADVERSE EFFECTS — overall favorable tolerability:
   a. Grade 1 PYREXIA: Patient experienced one episode of fever to ~38.5°C at approximately week 3, which is consistent with the class-effect dabrafenib-associated pyrexia. This is one of the most common toxicities seen with dabrafenib, occurring in up to 28% of patients on the doublet. Managed appropriately with one-day dose interruption and acetaminophen. He resumed without recurrence. Patient is counseled to continue to hold dabrafenib for any temperature ≥38.5°C per protocol and to contact the clinic. Given that this resolved promptly with a brief interruption, no dose reduction is warranted at this time. Patient was given written guidance on the pyrexia management protocol and standing acetaminophen prescription reviewed.
   b. Grade 1 PALMAR HYPERKERATOSIS: Bilateral palmar skin thickening is consistent with grade 1 palmar-plantar dysesthesia/keratoderma, a recognized skin toxicity of BRAF inhibitor therapy (distinct from hand-foot syndrome seen with other targeted agents). This is currently limited to mild thickening without pain, erosion, or functional impairment. Patient has been using recommended moisturizing cream; continue same. Urea-based emollient lotion prescribed. Dermatology referral placed for baseline skin surveillance (also for monitoring of keratoacanthomas/cuSCC risk — see below).
   c. Grade 1 FATIGUE: Mild and improving, not impairing daily function. No dose modification required.
   d. No QTc prolongation on today's ECG (QTc 428 ms). Cardiac monitoring to continue per protocol.
   e. LVEF stable at 60% on follow-up echocardiogram. No cardiac toxicity identified.
   f. No hepatotoxicity — LFTs improving from baseline (likely reflecting response of hepatic metastases rather than drug-induced liver injury).

3. CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA RISK: Patient is receiving a BRAF inhibitor (dabrafenib), which carries a known class-effect risk of cuSCC and keratoacanthoma development via paradoxical MAPK activation in RAS-mutant keratinocytes. While no suspicious lesions are present on examination today, this risk is ongoing and warrants proactive dermatologic surveillance. Monthly dermatology visits have been arranged.

4. PERFORMANCE STATUS IMPROVEMENT: Patient's ECOG performance status has improved from 1 at diagnosis to 0 today. This is likely a combination of: (a) rapid tumor response with associated symptom relief, and (b) psychological benefit of observing visible and palpable tumor regression. His overall trajectory at 6 weeks is highly favorable.

---

PLAN

1. TREATMENT: Continue dabrafenib 150 mg PO BID and trametinib 2 mg PO once daily at current doses. No dose modification indicated. Reinforce the importance of taking dabrafenib on an empty stomach (at least 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals), maintaining consistent 12-hour dosing intervals, and taking trametinib at a consistent time each day. Advise patient to avoid grapefruit and strong CYP3A4 inducers.

2. TOXICITY MONITORING:
   - Pyrexia management plan reviewed with patient; acetaminophen 500-650 mg q6h PRN reinforced; written instructions provided regarding dose holds (hold dabrafenib if temp ≥38.5°C, resume when afebrile x24 hours). Contact clinic for any persistent fever >38.5°C, rigors, or fever not resolving within 48 hours.
   - Palmar hyperkeratosis: urea 20% emollient cream twice daily to affected skin; follow up with dermatology.
   - Continue ECG every 4 weeks for QTc monitoring per protocol.
   - LFTs and LDH to be checked at next visit.
   - Echocardiogram every 3 months while on MEK inhibitor (trametinib) per cardiac monitoring guidelines.
   - Ophthalmology referral discussed — patient will self-report any visual changes; formal ophthalmology referral placed for slit-lamp exam at 3-month mark given trametinib-associated risk of uveitis and retinal vein occlusion (generally low risk but surveilled).

3. CUTANEOUS SURVEILLANCE: Dermatology consultation confirmed; patient to be seen monthly for full-body skin examination. Patient counseled to perform regular self-skin exams, to report any new rapidly growing or keratotic skin lesions promptly, and to maintain daily broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen given outdoor occupation. He has been advised to minimize direct UV exposure, wear sun-protective clothing, and consider UV-protective gloves during landscaping work.

4. RESTAGING IMAGING: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast scheduled at approximately 3 months from treatment initiation (approximately 6 weeks from today). Will compare to baseline CT. PET-CT not planned at first restaging; CT sufficient. Brain MRI to be revisited at 3-month mark depending on CT results and clinical status.

5. LABS AT NEXT VISIT: CBC, CMP (including LFTs), LDH, TSH. Consider glucose given rare dabrafenib-associated hyperglycemia.

6. PATIENT EDUCATION:
   - Reinforced education on the mechanism of acquired resistance — patient is aware that the median duration of response to BRAF/MEK inhibitors is approximately 9-12 months and that most patients will eventually develop resistance. His initial rapid response is encouraging.
   - Discussed that the treatment strategy involves using targeted therapy now for rapid control, with plans to eventually transition to immunotherapy (anti-PD-1 based) which may provide more durable benefit.
   - Encouraged patient to maintain open communication about any new or worsening symptoms.
   - Patient and spouse verbalized understanding of the plan. Questions answered.

7. RETURN TO CLINIC: 4 weeks for routine on-treatment monitoring visit. Earlier if fever, skin changes, visual changes, or other new symptoms develop.

CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
Restaging Examination — Cycle 3 Response Assessment

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Marcus R. Ellison, 55-year-old male with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV metastatic melanoma (M1c), primary site upper back. Known sites of disease include bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy with dominant right axillary conglomerate nodal mass, bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, multiple hepatic metastases (six lesions on baseline imaging, largest in hepatic segment VII), and numerous subcutaneous and cutaneous satellite nodules over the trunk and bilateral extremities. Patient initiated dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily and trametinib 2 mg once daily approximately three months prior to this examination. Prior clinical assessment at six weeks documented near-complete clinical resolution of palpable subcutaneous disease and marked LDH normalization. This examination is requested to assess radiographic treatment response to BRAF/MEK combination targeted therapy.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 120 mL of Omnipaque 350 (iohexol) intravenous contrast, acquired in portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal reformats were generated and reviewed. Images were compared with the prior staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis as well as the staging PET-CT. Slice thickness: 3 mm axial throughout.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The lungs are well-inflated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, consolidations, pleural effusions, or pneumothorax. The airways are patent to the segmental level. There is no endobronchial lesion. Interstitial pattern: none. Prior imaging had documented the absence of pulmonary metastases, and this remains unchanged.

Mediastinum:
The mediastinal contour is unremarkable. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The aortopulmonary window, subcarinal, and paratracheal stations contain no enlarged nodes. The central vessels are patent. No pericardial effusion. The heart is normal in size.

Axillary Regions:
Previously described right axillary conglomerate nodal mass has undergone marked and dramatic dimensional reduction. What was previously measured as a 4.8 x 3.9 cm lobulated conglomerate mass with areas of central necrosis now appears as a residual soft tissue nodal cluster measuring approximately 1.9 x 1.4 cm in its longest axial dimensions. The previously prominent central necrotic component is no longer appreciable; the residual lesion demonstrates relatively homogeneous soft tissue attenuation. No new right axillary adenopathy. Left axillary region: the small cluster of mildly enlarged left axillary nodes that were present on baseline imaging has resolved, with no discrete pathologic lymphadenopathy now identified.

Chest Wall / Soft Tissues of the Thorax:
Multiple subcutaneous soft tissue nodules were previously identified on the trunk and bilateral upper extremities. On today's examination, the majority of previously documented subcutaneous nodules are no longer identifiable. A few sites where previously discrete lesions were noted show no residual soft tissue abnormality. No new chest wall lesions.

CERVICAL EXTENSION (included on upper chest cuts):
The previously enlarged left cervical level II and level III lymph nodes have dramatically diminished. The largest previously measured at 2.2 cm; current imaging demonstrates residual nodes in the left level II region measuring no more than 0.8 cm in short axis, approaching normal morphology and size. No new cervical adenopathy detected on the included cuts.

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size without contour irregularity. Signal characteristics (attenuation) are appropriate for portal venous phase acquisition. Marked interval improvement is identified in the known hepatic metastatic disease.

The dominant hepatic segment VII lesion, previously measuring 3.1 x 2.7 cm and demonstrating irregular peripheral rim enhancement consistent with melanoma metastasis, now appears as a small, poorly marginated hypodense focus measuring approximately 1.1 cm in greatest dimension. The lesion retains subtle hypodensity relative to background hepatic parenchyma but lacks the distinct mass-like configuration and rim enhancement pattern seen on baseline. The reduction from 3.1 cm to 1.1 cm represents a change of approximately 65%.

The hepatic segment II lesion, previously measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm, has diminished to a small hypodense focus measuring 0.6 cm. This is near the lower threshold of reliable measurement but is noted as a discrete though diminutive hypodense area distinct from hepatic vessels.

The four additional smaller hepatic lesions documented at baseline (located in segments IV, V, VI, and VIII, ranging from 0.7 cm to 1.2 cm) are either no longer identifiable or have decreased to subcentimeter foci below reliable threshold for measurement. Specifically: one lesion previously 1.2 cm in segment IV is no longer identified with confidence. The lesion in segment V is questionably present as a 0.4 cm area of slight hypodensity. The remaining lesions in segments VI and VIII are not identifiable today.

No new hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein and hepatic veins are patent.

Gallbladder:
Normal, without evidence of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis.

Spleen:
Normal in size at approximately 10.8 cm. No focal splenic lesions. No perisplenic fluid.

Pancreas:
Unremarkable. Pancreatic duct not dilated. No pancreatic masses.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal. No adrenal masses or adenopathy. Previously there were no adrenal metastases identified, and this is unchanged.

Kidneys:
Bilateral kidneys are symmetric and enhance normally. No hydronephrosis, renal masses, or perinephric fluid collections. A 4 mm non-enhancing hypodense focus in the right kidney lower pole is noted, too small to characterize but most consistent with a simple cyst.

Retroperitoneum / Mesentery:
No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No free fluid in the abdomen.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
The visualized bowel is normal in caliber throughout. No bowel wall thickening, obstruction, or mesenteric stranding. Appendix is not enlarged.

Abdominal Wall:
Previously noted subcutaneous nodules over the abdominal wall and trunk have largely resolved. No discrete residual abdominal wall subcutaneous masses are identified. One area on the right lateral flank at approximately the level of the iliac crest demonstrates a small residual soft tissue thickening measuring approximately 4 mm, which is indeterminate but markedly reduced compared to the prior scan.

PELVIS:

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. The iliac chains are clear. No new nodal disease in the pelvis.

Pelvic Organs:
The bladder is normally distended. The prostate is normal in size and contour without nodularity. Seminal vesicles are unremarkable. No pelvic free fluid.

Bony Structures:
The visualized bony structures of the spine, pelvis, ribs, and sternum demonstrate no aggressive lytic or sclerotic lesions to suggest osseous metastatic involvement. This is consistent with prior imaging, which did not document bony disease.

SUBCUTANEOUS / SOFT TISSUE OVERVIEW:
Systematic review of subcutaneous tissues throughout the imaged region demonstrates near-complete resolution of the numerous subcutaneous satellite nodules previously documented. On the staging CT these ranged from 0.8 to 1.4 cm and were present at multiple sites across the trunk and extremities; essentially all such lesions are below the threshold of detection on today's examination or are entirely absent. No new subcutaneous lesions.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Dramatic and extensive treatment response across all previously documented disease sites following approximately three months of dabrafenib and trametinib combination targeted therapy. The changes are most consistent with a marked partial response to BRAF/MEK inhibitor treatment.

2. Right axillary nodal conglomerate mass markedly reduced, from 4.8 cm down to 1.9 cm, with resolution of the central necrotic component. The residual soft tissue likely represents a combination of fibrosis, necrosis, and residual viable tumor; however, the degree of reduction is striking.

3. Hepatic metastases show impressive treatment response. The dominant segment VII lesion has shrunk from 3.1 cm to approximately 1.1 cm. The segment II lesion has regressed from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm. The majority of the remaining smaller hepatic lesions are no longer identifiable on today's examination, consistent with resolution or regression below the detection threshold.

4. Subcutaneous and cutaneous satellite nodules have undergone near-complete resolution throughout the trunk and extremities — findings that are concordant with the clinical examination, which previously documented near-complete resolution of palpable disease.

5. Left cervical lymphadenopathy markedly reduced, with nodal dimensions approaching normal size limits.

6. No new lesions identified. No pulmonary metastases, no adrenal involvement, no osseous metastatic disease, no CNS lesions (outside the scope of this examination; dedicated brain MRI recommended if not recent).

7. Overall radiographic picture is consistent with a robust early response to targeted BRAF/MEK inhibition, reflecting the well-established pattern of rapid but frequently time-limited disease control seen in BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma treated with this regimen. Clinical correlation with LDH trending is recommended. Continued surveillance imaging at the treating oncologist's discretion.
